question: The ratio of the radii of two planets is $r$ and the ratio of accelerations due to gravity on the planets is $x$. Then the ratio of the escape velocities from the planets is
explanation:
Given,
Using the formula for escape velocity
$v=\sqrt{2 R g}$
Let planet 1 has $R_{1}$ radius and $g_{1}$ acceleration due to gravity.
For planet $2, R_{2}$ and $g_{2}$.
${v_1} = \sqrt {2{R_1}{g_1}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
${v_2} = \sqrt {2{R_2}{g_2}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2)
$\dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 R_{1} g_{1}}{2 R_{2} g_{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\sqrt{r x} \quad\left[r=\dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}, x=\dfrac{g_{1}}{g_{2}}\right]$
Hence, ratio of escape velocities are $\sqrt{r x}$.
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A rocket is fired vertically with a speed of $4 {~km} / {s}$ from the earth's surface. How far does it go before returning back to the earth? (Take, radius of earth $=6.4 \times 10^{6} {~m}$ and $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952621/preptracker/questions/q_109708_explanation_1764952620788.png By using conservation of energy, we have
$\therefore-\dfrac{G m M_{e}}{(R+h)}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\left[\dfrac{-G m M_{e}}{R}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{-G m M_{e}}{(R+h)}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\dfrac{G m M_{e}}{R}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{G m M_{e}}{R}-\dfrac{G m M_{e}}{R+h}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} v^{2}=G M_{e}\left[\dfrac{1}{R}-\dfrac{1}{R+h}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} \times(4000)^{2}=\dfrac{G M_{e}}{R^{2}} \times R^{2}\left[\dfrac{R+h-R}{R(R+h)}\right]$
$\Rightarrow 8 \times 10^{6}=g R^{2}\left[\dfrac{h}{R(R+h)}\right]$
$\Rightarrow 8 \times 10^{6}=\dfrac{g h R}{(R+h)}=\dfrac{10 h R}{(R+h)}$
$\Rightarrow 8 \times 10^{5}=\dfrac{h R}{R+h}$
$\Rightarrow 8 \times 10^{5}(R+h)=h R$
$\Rightarrow 8 \times 10^{5}\left[6.4 \times 10^{6}+h\right]=h \times 6.4 \times 10^{6}$
$\Rightarrow 51.2 \times 10^{11}=\left(6.4 \times 10^{6}-8 \times 10^{5}\right) h$
$\Rightarrow 51.2 \times 10^{11}=\left(64 \times 10^{5}-8 \times 10^{5}\right) h$
$\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{51.2 \times 10^{11}}{56 \times 10^{5}}=0.914 \times 10^{6} {~m}$
$\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{0.914 \times 10^{6}}{10^{3}} {~km}=914 {~km}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: Let the escape speed of an object on the earth's surface be $v_{0}$. The object is projected out with a speed $5 v_{0}$. The speed of the object far away from the earth will be
explanation:
The situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952612/preptracker/questions/q_109705_explanation_1764952612469.png Escape speed is $v_{0}$,
$ \Rightarrow {v_0} = \sqrt {\frac{{2GM}}{R}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Now, if velocity of object at infinity is $v$, then by conservation of energy we have,
Energy at surface $=$ Energy at infinity
$\dfrac{-G M m}{R}+\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(5 v_{0}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+0$
Now, $\quad \dfrac{G M}{R}=\dfrac{1}{2} v_{0}^{2}$ [from Eq. (1)]
So, $\quad-\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(5 v_{0}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad-v_{0}^{2}+25 v_{0}^{2}=v^{2}$ or $v=\sqrt{24\left(v_{0}^{2}\right)}$
or $\quad v=2 \sqrt{6} v_{0}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: If the escape velocity from the surface of the earth is $11.2 {~km} / {s}$, its value from the surface of a planet having double the radius and 8 time the mass of earth is
explanation:
Escape velocity of an object on a planetary body,
$v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}} \Rightarrow v_{e} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{R}}$
So, $\dfrac{\left(v_{e}\right)_{1}}{\left(v_{e}\right)_{2}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{\dfrac{M_{1}}{R_{1}}}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{M_{2}}{R_{2}}}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\left(v_{e}\right)_{1}}{\left(v_{e}\right)_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{M_{1}}{M_{2}} \times \dfrac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}}$
$\dfrac{11.2}{\left(v_{e}\right)_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{M}{8 M} \times \dfrac{2 R}{R}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{11.2}{\left(v_{e}\right)_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{4}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{11.2}{\left(v_{e}\right)_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow\left(v_{e}\right)_{2}=22.4 {~km} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: Consider a spherical planet which is rotating about its axis such that the escape speed of a point on its equator is $v$ and the effective acceleration due to gravity on the equator is $1 / 3$ of its value at the poles. What is the escape speed for a particle at the pole of this planet?
explanation:
Escape velocity on the surface of a planet is given by
${v_e} = \sqrt {\frac{{2GM}}{R}} = \sqrt {\frac{{2g{R^2}}}{R}} \quad \left[ {\because GM = g{R^2}} \right]$
$ = \sqrt {2gR} $
Given, the velocity at equator, $v_{B}=v$ and acceleration due to gravity at equator,
${g_E} = \frac{1}{3}{g_P} \Rightarrow {g_P} = 3{g_E}\quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
The escape velocity of a particle at equator,
${v_E} = \sqrt {2{g_E}R} \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
and at poles,
${v_P} = \sqrt {2{g_P}R} $
$ = \sqrt {2 \times 3{g_E}R} = \sqrt 3 \sqrt {2{g_E}R} \quad \quad $ [From Eq. (1)]
$ = \sqrt 3 {v_E}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad $ [From Eq. (2)]
${ = \sqrt 3 v\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ {\because {v_E} = v} \right]}$
Hence, $\sqrt{3} v$ is the escape velocity for a particle at the pole of this planet.
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: If a planet of mass $6.4 \times 10^{23} {~kg}$ can be compressed into a sphere such that the escape velocity from its surface becomes $8 \times 10^{4} {~m} / {s}$, then what should be the radius of the compressed sphere? (Gravitational constant,$G=6.6 \times 10^{-11} {Nm}^{-2} {~kg}^{-2}$)
explanation:
As, we know, the escape velocity of a planet is given as
$\nu_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}}$ or $\nu_{e}^{2}=\dfrac{2 G M}{R}$
Radius of sphere $=\dfrac{2 G M}{v_{e}^{2}}$
$ \Rightarrow R = \frac{{2 \times 6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 11}} \times 6.4 \times {{10}^{23}}}}{{64 \times {{10}^8}}}$
$ = 13.2 \times {10^3}\,m$
$\Rightarrow R=13.2 {~km}$
Hence, option (2) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: If the acceleration due to gravity $g$ doubles and the radius of earth becomes half that of the present value, then the value of escape velocity is approximately (Assume, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ and radius of earth, $R=6400 {~km}$)
explanation:
Escape velocity from the surface of earth,
$v_{e}=\sqrt{2 g R_{e}}=\sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 6.4 \times 10^{6}}$
$=11.3 \times 10^{3} {~m} / {s}$
Again, when $g^{\prime}=2 g$ and $R^{\prime}=\dfrac{R}{2}$
Now, escape velocity is given by
$v_{e}^{\prime}=\sqrt{2 g^{\prime} R^{\prime}}=\sqrt{2 \times 2 g \times \dfrac{R}{2}}=\sqrt{2 g R}=v_{c}$
$=11.3 \times 10^{3} {~m} / {s}=11.3 {~km} / {s} \approx 12 {~km} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: The mass of the moon is $\dfrac{1}{144}$ times the mass ofa planet and its diameter is $\dfrac{1}{16}$ times the  diameter of a planet. If the escape velocity on the planet is $v$, the escape velocity on the moon will be
explanation:
Escapee velocity on the planet,
$v = \sqrt {\frac{{2GM}}{R}} = \sqrt {\frac{{4GM}}{D}} \quad \quad (1)$
Where, $M=$ Mass of the planet
$R=$ Radius of the planet
$G=$ Universal gravitational constant
$D=$ Diameter of the planet
$v \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{D}}$
For moon, $v' = \sqrt {\frac{{2GM'}}{{R'}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{4GM'}}{{D'}}} \quad \quad (2)$
where, $M^{\prime}=$ mass of the moon
$R^{\prime}=$ Radius of the moon;
$D^{\prime}=$ diameter of the moon
Given : $M^{\prime}=\dfrac{M}{144}$ and $D^{\prime}=\dfrac{D}{16}$
From equation (1) and (2), we get
$\frac{{v'}}{v} = \sqrt {\frac{{M'D}}{{D'M}}} ;\frac{{v'}}{v} = \sqrt {\frac{{\frac{M}{{\frac{{144}}{D}}} \times \frac{D}{1}}}{{16}} \times \frac{M}{1}} $
$=\dfrac{1}{3} \Rightarrow v^{\prime}=\dfrac{v}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: Escape velocity of a body from earth is  $11.2\,km/s$. If the radius of a planet be one-third the radius of earth and mass be one-sixth that  of earth, the escape velocity from the planet is
explanation:
Escape velocity on the earth,
${v_e} = \sqrt {\frac{{2GM}}{R}} = 11.2\,\,km{s^{ - 1}}$
Escape velocity on the planet, $v_{p}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M^{\prime}}{R^{\prime}}}$
For the planet, $M^{\prime}=\dfrac{M}{6}, R^{\prime}=\dfrac{R}{3}$
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{2GM/6}}{{R/3}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{GM}}{R}} = 7.9km{s^{ - 1}}$
$\left( {\because \sqrt {\frac{{2Gm}}{R}} = 11.2\,m/s} \right)$
subject: Physics

question: To project a body of mass $m$ from earth's surface to infinity, the required kinetic energy is (assume, the radius of earth is $R_{E}, g=$ acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth)
explanation:
Kinetic energy required to project a body of mass $m$ from earth's surface to infinity is given by $E_{i}=E_{f}$
$\Rightarrow K_{i}+U_{i}=K_{f}+U_{f}=0$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore K_{i}=-U_{i}=-\left(-m g R_{E}\right) \\& \quad=m g R_{E}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The escape velocity for a body projected vertically upwards from the earth's surface is ${11 {~km} / {s}}$. If the body is projected at an angle of ${45^{\circ}}$ with the vertical, then, the escape velocity will be
explanation:
Escape velocity is independent of the angle of projectile.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: For a satellite, escape velocity is ${11 {~km} / {s}}$. If the satellite is launched at an angle of ${60^{\circ}}$ with the vertical, then escape velocity will be
explanation:
${v_{{esc}}=11 {~km} / {s}, \theta=60^{\circ}}$
${v_{\text {esc }}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}}}$
As escape velocity is independent of the angle projectile.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: Two planets $A$ and $B$ have the same material density. If the radius of $A$ is twice that of $B$, then the ratio of the escape velocity $v_{A} / v_{B}$ is
explanation:
${v_e} = \sqrt {2gR} = \sqrt {\frac{{2GM}}{{{R^2}}}R} = \sqrt {\frac{{2Gd\frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3}}}{{{R^2}}}} R$
$ = R\sqrt {2Gd\frac{4}{3}\pi } $
as ${v_e} \propto R$ for same density, $\frac{{{v_A}}}{{{v_B}}} = 2$
subject: Physics

question: Escape velocity from a planet is $v_{e}$. If its mass is increased to 8 times and its radius is increased to 2 times, then the new escape velocity would be
explanation:
Escape velocity of a body on earth surface is given by
$v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}}$
where, $G$ is gravitational constant, $M$ is mass of planet and $R$ is radius.
When $M^{\prime}=8 M, R^{\prime}=2 R$, then
$\Rightarrow v_{e}^{\prime}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G(8 M)}{(2 R)}}=2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}}$
$\Rightarrow v_{e}^{\prime}=2 v_{e}$
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of escape velocity at earth $\left(v_{e}\right)$ to the escape velocity at a planet $\left(v_{p}\right)$ whose radius and mean density are twice as that of earth is
explanation:
Since, the escape velocity of earth can be given as
${v_e} = \sqrt {2gR} = R\sqrt {\frac{8}{3}\pi G\rho } $
$\left[ {\because g = \frac{4}{3}\pi G\rho R} \right]$
$[\rho = {\text{ density }}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{ earth }}]$
$ \Rightarrow {v_e} = R\sqrt {\frac{8}{3}\pi G\rho } \quad \quad \quad (1)$
As it is given that, the radius and mean density of planet are twice as that of earth. So, escape velocity at planet will be
${v_p} = 2\,R\sqrt {\frac{8}{3}\,\,\pi \,G\,2p} \quad \quad \quad (2)$
Divide Eq. (1) by Eq. (2), we get
$\dfrac{v_{e}}{v_{p}}=\dfrac{R \sqrt{\dfrac{8}{3} \pi G \rho}}{2 R \sqrt{\dfrac{8}{3} \pi G 2 \rho}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{e}}{v_{p}}=\dfrac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: If escape velocity on earth surface is $11.1 \mathrm{kmh}^{-1}$, then find the escape velocity on moon surface. If mass of moon is $\dfrac{1}{81}$ times of mass of earth and radius of moon is $\dfrac{1}{4}$ times radius of earth
explanation:
Given, Escape velocity on the surface of earth, ${v_e} = 11.1\,\,km/h$
i.e, $\quad {v_e} = \sqrt {2g{R_e}} $
${v_e} = \sqrt {\frac{{2G{M_e}}}{{{R_e}}}} $
i.e. Mass of moon, $M_{m}=\dfrac{M_{e}}{81}$
Radius of moon, $R_{m}=\dfrac{R_{e}}{4}$
$\therefore$ Escape velocity on the surface of moon
$\begin{aligned}& v_{m}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M_{m}}{R_{m}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G \dfrac{M e}{81}}{\dfrac{R_{e}}{4}}} \\& =\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2}}{9} \sqrt{\dfrac{G M_{e}}{R_{e}}} \\& =\dfrac{2}{9} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M_{e}}{R_{e}}}=\dfrac{2}{9} v_{e} \\& =\dfrac{2}{9} \times 11.1=2.46 k m h^{-1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The escape velocities of two planets $A$ and $B$ are in the ratio $1: 2$. If the ratio of their radii respectively is $1: 3$, then the ratio of acceleration due to gravity of planet $A$ to the acceleration of gravity of planet $B$ will be
explanation:
Given, $\dfrac{v_{A}}{v_{B}}=\dfrac{1}{2}, \dfrac{R_{A}}{R_{B}}=\dfrac{1}{3}, \dfrac{g_{A}}{g_{B}}=$?
Escape velocity is given by, $v_{e}=\sqrt{2 g R}$
$v_{e} \propto \sqrt{g R}$
$\Rightarrow g \propto \dfrac{V e^{2}}{R}$
So, $\frac{{{v_A}}}{{{v_B}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{g_A}{R_A}}}{{{g_B}{R_B}}}} $
$ \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{{{g_A}}}{{{g_B}}} \times \frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{{{g_A}}}{{{g_B}}} = \frac{3}{4}$
subject: Physics

question: If earth has a mass nine times and radius twice to that of a planet $P$. Then $\frac{{{v_e}}}{3}\sqrt x \,m{s^{ - 1}}$ will be the minimum velocity required by a rocket to pull out of gravitational force of $P$, where $v_{e}$ is escape velocity on earth. The value of $x$ is
explanation:
Here, $M_{e}=9 M_{p}, R_{e}=2 R_{P}, v_{P}=\dfrac{v_{e} \sqrt{x}}{3}$
Escape speed for earth is given by
${v_e} = \sqrt {\frac{{G{M_e}}}{{{R_e}}}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
So, for planet $P,{v_P} = \sqrt {\frac{{G{M_P}}}{{{R_P}}}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$\therefore$ Dividing equation (2) by (1),
$\dfrac{v_{P}}{v_{e}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{M_{P}}{M_{e}} \times \dfrac{R_{e}}{R_{P}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{M_{P}}{9 M_{P}} \times \dfrac{2 R_{P}}{R_{P}}}$
$=\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{3} \Rightarrow v_{P}=v_{e} \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{3}=\dfrac{\sqrt{x}}{3} v_{e}$
So, $x=2$
subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below :
For a planet, if the ratio of mass of the planet to its radius increases, the escape velocity from the planet also increases.
Escape velocity is independent of the radius of the planet.
explanation:
As, the escape velocity is given by,
$v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}}$
where $\mathrm{M}=$ Mass of planet
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{R}} = $ Radius of planet
If ratio of mass to radius of planet $\left(\dfrac{M}{R}\right)$ increases, the escape velocity also increases. Escape velocity is inversely proportional to square root of radius of planet and directly proportional to $\sqrt{M}$
$v_{e} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{R}}$
So, statement $\mathrm{A}$ is correct but statement $\mathrm{B}$ is incorrect.
So, correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are correct.
Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.
Both Statement A and Statement B are incorrect.

subject: Physics

question: A planet having mass $9 M_{e}$ and radius $4 R_{e}$, where $M_{e}$ and $R_{e}$ are mass and radius of earth respectively, has escape velocity in $km/s$ given by (Given escape velocity on earth ${V_e} = 11.2 \times {10^3}\,m/s$)
explanation:
Given, $M_{p}=9 M_{e} ; R_{p}=4 R_{e}$
Escape velocity is given by
$v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M_{e}}{R_{e}}}$
$v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M_{p}}{R_{p}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G \times 9 M_{e}}{4 R_{e}}}$
$=\sqrt{\dfrac{G M_{e}}{R_{e}} \times \dfrac{9}{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{9}{4}} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M_{e}}{R_{e}}}$
$ = \frac{3}{2} \times {v_e} = \left( {\frac{3}{2} \times 11.2} \right)km/s$
${v_e} = (3 \times 5.6)km/s = 16.8\,km/s$
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of escape velocity of a planet to the escape velocity of earth will be (Given: Mass of the planet is 16 times mass of earth and radius of the planet is 4 times the radius of earth)
explanation:
As, $v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}} ; v_{p}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G(16 M)}{4 R}}$
$v_{e} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{R}}$
$\dfrac{v_{p}}{v_{e}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G(16 M)}{4 R} \times \dfrac{R}{2 G M}}$
$\dfrac{v_{p}}{v_{e}}=\dfrac{2}{1}=2: 1$
So, correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Earth has an atmosphere but the moon does not.

Reason: Smaller escape velocity in the moon makes the moon to have no atmosphere.
explanation:
The escape velocity of a gas on the surface of the Moon is relatively low, only $2.5\,km{\rm{/}}s$.$v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}}$.
$M$ is smaller for moon as a result, all the gas molecules have escaped, leading to the absence of an atmosphere on the Moon. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A stationary object is released from a point $P$ at a distance $3 R$ from the centre of the Moon which has radius $R$ and Mass $M$. Which one of the following expressions gives the speed of the object on hitting the Moon?
explanation:
$U_{i}+K_{i}=U_{f}+K_{f}$
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{-G M m}{3 R}+0=\dfrac{-G M m}{R}+\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{f}^{2} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v_{f}^{2}=\dfrac{G M m}{R}-\dfrac{G M m}{3 R}\end{aligned}$
$\dfrac{1}{2} v_{f}^{2}=\dfrac{2}{3} \dfrac{G M}{R} \Rightarrow v_{f}=\left(\dfrac{4}{3} \dfrac{G M}{R}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of escape velocities of two planets if $g$ value on the two planets are $9.9\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$ and $3.3\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$ and their radii are $6400\,km$ and $3200\,km$ respectively is
subject: Physics

question: The escape velocity of a body from the surface of the earth is $11.2\,km{\rm{/}}s$. The escape velocity of a body from a planet having same mean density as the earth but twice the radius of earth is
explanation:
Escape velocity, $v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M_{e}}{R_{e}}}$
$=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G \cdot \rho \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R_{e}^{3}}{R_{e}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{8}{3}} \rho R_{e}^{2} \cdot G$
$\Rightarrow v_{e} \alpha \sqrt{R_{e}^{2}} \Rightarrow v_{e} \propto R_{e}$
Now, as radius of planet is twice that of earth then, $R=2 R_{e}$
So escape velocity of body from planet,
$v = 2{R_e} = 2 \times 11.2\,km{\rm{/}}s$
$ = 22.4\,km{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics

question: The moon has a mass of $1 / 81$ that of the earth and a radius of $1 / 4$ that of the earth. The escape speed from the surface of the earth is $11.2\,km{\rm{/}}s$. The escape speed from the surface of the moon is:
explanation:
The escape velocity $\left(V_{e}\right)=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M_{e}}{R_{e}}}$
$V_{m}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M_{e}}{\dfrac{81}{\dfrac{R_{e}}{4}}}}$
$V_{m}=\dfrac{2}{9} V_{e}$
${V_m} = \frac{2}{9} \times 11.2 = 2.49\,km{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics

question: A spaceship is launched into a circular orbit close to earth's surface. What additional velocity has now to be imparted to the spaceship in the orbit to overcome the gravitational pull? (Radius of earth $ = 6400\,km$ and $g = 9.8\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
The speed of sattelite very close to earth is nearly $8\,km{s^{ - 1}}$.
The additional velocity required to make it to escape is nearly $3.2\,km{s^{ - 1}}$.
subject: Physics

question: The earth retains its atmosphere. This is due to

options.text: The special shape of the earth
The escape velocity being greater than the mean speed of the molecules of the atmospheric gases.
The escape velocity being smaller than the mean speed of the molecules of the atmospheric gases.

subject: Physics

question: Four equal masses (each of mass $m$ ) are placed at the corners of a squal of side $a$. The escape velocity of a body from the centre $O$ of the square is
explanation:
Potential energy of the particle is
$U = - 4\left( {\frac{{GMm}}{{\frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}}}} \right)$
$\therefore - 4\left( {\frac{{GMm}}{{a/\sqrt 2 }}} \right) + \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = 0 \Rightarrow v = \sqrt {\frac{{8\sqrt 2 GM}}{a}} $
subject: Physics

question: The escape velocity for a planet is ${v_e}$. A tunnel is dug along a diameter of the planet and a small body is dropped into it from the surface. When the body reaches the centre of the planet, its speed will be
explanation:
From conservation of mechanical energy.
$ - \frac{{GMm}}{R} + 0 = - \frac{{3GMm}}{{2R}} + \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
$v = \sqrt {\frac{{GM}}{R}} = \frac{{{v_e}}}{{\sqrt 2 }}$
subject: Physics

question: A missile is launched with a velocity less than the escape velocity. The sum of its kinetic and potential energies is
explanation:
When a body is projected with escape velocity then $T.E$ is zero at infinity.
$\dfrac{-G M m}{R}+\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{e}^{2}=0$
If the initial velocity is less than $v_{e}$ then the body is in bound with earth and hence $T.E$ $=(-)$ ve

options.text: May be positive or negative depending upon its initial velocity

subject: Physics

question: The velocity with which a projectile must be fired so that it escapes earth's gravitation does not depend on:
explanation:
The escape velocity of a body is
$\therefore \quad v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M_{e}}{R_{e}}}$
The above formula shows that escape velocity is independent of the mass of the projectile.
subject: Physics

question: If $v_{e}$ is escape velocity and $v_{0}$, is orbital velocity of a satellite for orbit close to the earth's surface. Then they are related by
explanation:
Escape velocity,
${v_e} = \sqrt {\frac{{2GM}}{R}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Where $M$ and $R$ be the mass and radius of the earth respectively.
The orbital velocity of a satellite close to the earth's surface is
${v_0} = \sqrt {\frac{{GM}}{R}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2), we get $v_{e}=\sqrt{2} v_{0}$
subject: Physics

question: The masses and the radius of the earth and the moon are $M_{1}, M_{2}$ and $R_{1}, R_{2}$ respectively their centres are at distance $d$ apart. The minimum speed with which a particle of mass $m$ should be projected form a point midway between the two centres so as to escape to infinity will be
explanation: The $P.E.$ of the mass at $d$/2 distance from the earth and moon is    https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955818/preptracker/questions/q_34880_explanation_1764955817962.png    $\begin{gathered}U=-2 \dfrac{G M_{1} m}{d}-2 \dfrac{G M_{2} m}{d} \\U=-\dfrac{2 G m}{d}\left(M_{1}+M_{2}\right) \text { (Numerically) } \\\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{e}^{2}=U \Rightarrow v_{e}=2 \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{d}\left(M_{1}+M_{2}\right)}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics

question: There is no atmosphere on moon because
explanation: As, the escape velocity on the surface of moon's is small i.e., 2.4 $km/s$ in comparision to 11.2 $km/s$ that of earth and also this velocity is smaller than $rms$ velocity of gas molecules, therefore gas molecules escape moon's gravitation. Thus, moon cannot have atmosphere.

options.text: The escape velocity of the gas molecules is less than their root mean square velocity here

subject: Physics

question: The condition for a uniform spherical mass $M$ of radius $r$ to be a black hole is $[G=$ gravitational constant and $g=$ acceleration due to gravity]
explanation:
Escape velocity for that body $v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{r}}$
$v_{e}$ should be more than or equal to speed of light i.e., $\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{r}} \geq c$
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of the radii of planets $A$ and $B$ is $k_{1}$ and ratio of acceleration due to gravity on them is $k_{2}$. The ratio of escape velocities from them will be
explanation:
The escape velocity is
$\begin{gathered}v=\sqrt{2 g R} \\\Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{A}}{v_{B}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g_{A}}{g_{B}} \times \dfrac{R_{A}}{R_{B}}}=\sqrt{k_{1} \times k_{2}}=\sqrt{k_{1} k_{2}}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics

question: A body of mass $m$ is situated at a distance $4 R_{e}$ above the earth's surface, where $R_{e}$ is the radius of earth. How much minimum energy be given to the body so that it may escape
explanation:
Potential energy of the body at a distance $4 R_{e}$ from the surface of earth
$U=\dfrac{-G M m}{5 R_{e}}=-\dfrac{m g R_{e}}{5}$
Minimum energy required to escape the body will be
$\dfrac{m g R_{e}}{5}$
subject: Physics

question: A black hole is an object whose gravitational field is so strong that even light cannot escape from it. To what approximate radius would earth (mass $\left. { = 5.98 \times {{10}^{24}}\,kg} \right)$ have to be compressed to be a black hole?
explanation:
$v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}}=C$
$R=\dfrac{2 G M}{C^{2}}=\dfrac{2 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 5.98 \times 10^{24}}{\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)^{2}}$
$ = \frac{{2 \times 6.67 \times 5.98}}{9} \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m$
$ = 8.86 \times {10^{ - 3}} \approx {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
subject: Physics

question:
Moon has no atmosphere.
The escape velocity for moon is less than that for Earth.
explanation: On earth escape velocity is 11.2 $km/s$ whereas on moon it is 2.4 $km/s$. Moon does not have atmosphere. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Two different planets can have same escape velocity

Reason: Value of escape velocity is a universal constant.
explanation: As, escape velocity $=\sqrt{\dfrac{G M}{R}}$, so its value depends on mass of planet and radius of the planet. The two different planets have same escape velocity, when mass and radius are equal for both planets. So option (3) is correct

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: The ratio of escape velocity at earth $\left(v_{e}\right)$ to the escape velocity at a planet $\left(v_{p}\right)$ whose radius and mean density are twice as that of earth is:-
explanation:
The escape velocity
$\begin{aligned}& v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G}{R}\left(\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} \rho\right)} \propto R \sqrt{\rho} \\& \dfrac{v_{e}}{v_{P}}=\dfrac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The escape velocity from the Earth's surface is $v$. The escape velocity from the surface of another planet having a radius, four times that of Earth and same mass density is :
explanation:
$v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}}$
$=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} \rho}{R}}$

$\begin{gathered}=R \sqrt{\dfrac{8}{3} \pi G \rho} \\v_{e} \propto R \\\dfrac{v}{v_{P}}=\dfrac{R}{4 R} \\v_{P}=4 v\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics

question: The escape velocity from the surface of the Earth is (where $R_{E}$ is the radius of the earth)
explanation:
The escape velocity form the surface of the Earth is
$v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M_{E}}{R_{E}}}=\sqrt{2 g R_{E}}\left(\because g=\dfrac{G M_{E}}{R_{E}^{2}}\right)$
subject: Physics

question: The escape speed of a body on the Earth's surface is $11.2\,km\,{s^{ - 1}}$. A body is projected with thrice of this speed. The speed of the body when it escapes the gravitational pull of Earth is
explanation:
Let $v$ be the speed of the body when it escapes the gravitational pull of the Earth and $u$ be speed of projection of the body from the Earth's surface.
According to law of conservation of mechanical energy,
$\dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2}-\dfrac{G M_{E} m}{R_{E}}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-0$
where $m$ and $M_{E}$ be masses of the body and Earth respectively and $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}$ is the radius of the Earth.
or $v^{2}=u^{2}-\dfrac{2 G M_{E}}{R_{E}}$
$v^{2}=u^{2}-v_{e}^{2} \quad\left(\because v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M_{E}}{R_{E}}}\right)$
$v=\sqrt{u^{2}-v_{e}^{2}}=\sqrt{\left(3 v_{e}\right)^{2}-v_{e}^{2}}$
$ = \sqrt 8 {v_e} = 11.2\sqrt 8 \,km\,{s^{ - 1}} = 22.4\sqrt 2 \,km\,{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: A space station is at a height equal to the radius of the Earth. If $v_{E}$ is the escape velocity on the surface of the Earth, the same on the space station is_____times $v_{E}$
explanation:
As the space station is at a height $h$ from the surface of the earth, its distance from the centre of the Earth is $(R + h)$
Thus, the escape velocity on the space station is
$\begin{aligned}& v_{s}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{(R+h)}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{(R+R)}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{2 R}}(\text { as } h=R) \\= & \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}}\right)=\dfrac{v_{E}}{\sqrt{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A body is projected vertically from the surface of the earth of radius $'R'$ with velocity equal to half of the escape velocity. The maximum height reached by the body is
explanation:
According to the law of conservation of energy
$\begin{aligned}& (K E+P E)_{\text {surface }}=(K E+P E)_{(\max \text { height })} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{R}\right)=0+\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{R+h}\right)\end{aligned}$
Given, $\quad v=\dfrac{1}{2} v_{e}=\dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m\left[\dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \dfrac{2 G M}{R}\right]-\dfrac{G M m}{R}=-\dfrac{G M m}{R+h} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{4} \dfrac{G M m}{R}-\dfrac{G M m}{R}=-\dfrac{G M m}{R+h} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{3}{4 R}=\dfrac{1}{R+h} \\& \Rightarrow h=\dfrac{R}{3}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The escape velocity of a body from any planet, whose mass is six times the mass of earth and radius is twice the radius of earth will be $\left(v_{e}=\right.$ escape velocity of a body from the earth's surface)
explanation:
The escape velocity of body from earth's surface is
$v_{e}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}}$
Where, $G$ is universal gravitational constant, $M$ is mass of earth and $R$ is radius of earth.
For the planet $M_{1}=6 M$ and $R_{1}=2 R$.
Therefore, the escape velocity from the planet will be
$v_{p}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G(6 M)}{(2 R)}}=\sqrt{3} v_{e}$
subject: Physics

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The gravitational force inside a hollow spherical shell is zero.

Reason: According to the shell theorem, a uniform spherical shell of matter attracts a particle that is outside the shell as if all the shell’s mass were concentrated at its center.
explanation: The assertion is true because the gravitational force at any point inside a hollow spherical shell is zero, which is a result of the shell theorem. The reason is also correct as it correctly states the shell theorem's conclusion regarding the force outside the shell. Correct option is (1).

explanation: The Reason given is a correct explanation of the Assertion, which is a consequence of the shell theorem. Correct option is (1)

question:
Read the  Statement - A and  Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below :
The force of attraction due to a hollow spherical shell of uniform density on a point mass situated inside it is always positive.
The force of attraction between a hollow spherical shell of uniform density and a point mass situated outside is same just as, if the entire mass of the shell is at the centre of the shell. Which of the following is correct?
explanation: Inside a hollow spherical shell, net gravitational field is zero at all the points. Hence, force of attraction on a point mass inside shell is always zero. Spherical objects like shells acts like a point mass at centre. This is shell theorem. So statement B is correct. So, the correct option is (3).

Statement B is true, but statement A is false
Both statement A and statement B are false

question: A ring has a mass $M$ and radius $R$. The distance of the point on its geometric axis from its centre at which the gravitational field is strongest, is
explanation:
Given, Mass of ring $=m$, Radius $=R$
We have to calculate the distance from its geometrical axis Gravitational field at point $P$ is given by at point $P$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952627/preptracker/questions/q_109715_explanation_1764952627153.png ${E_P} = \frac{{GMx}}{{{{\left( {{R^2} + {x^2}} \right)}^{3/2}}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
To get the maximum value of $E_{P}$
$\dfrac{d E_{p}}{d x}=0$
$ \Rightarrow \left[ \begin{gathered} \frac{{GM}}{{{{\left( {{R^2} + {x^2}} \right)}^{3/2}}}} - GMx \times \hfill \\ \frac{3}{{2{{\left( {{R^2} + {x^2}} \right)}^{1/2}}}} \times 2x \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right] = 0$
Solving above expression,
$3 x^{2}=R^{2}+x^{2}$
$2 x^{2}=R^{2} \Rightarrow x= \pm \dfrac{R}{\sqrt{2}}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Field Due to Non spherical Bodies

question: A uniform sphere $A$ with radius $R$ exerts a force $F$ on a small particle $B$ situated at a distance $2 R$ from the centre of the sphere. A spherical portion of diameter $R$ is cut from the sphere $A$ as shown in the figure. If $F^{\prime}$ is the new gravitational force between the remaining part of the sphere $A$ and the particle $B$, then the correct relation between $F$ and $F^{\prime}$ is
explanation:
The force of attraction between the complete sphere of mass $M$ and particle of mass $m$ is
$F = \frac{{GmM}}{{4{R^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Mass of the complete sphere, $M=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} \rho$
where, $\rho$ is density of sphere.
Mass of the cut out portion,
$m^{\prime}=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi\left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)^{3} \rho=\dfrac{M}{8}$
Now, the distance between the centre of the cut out portion and mass $m$,
$=2 R-\dfrac{R}{2}=\dfrac{3 R}{2}$
Hence, the force of attraction between the cut out portion and mass $m$ is
${F^\prime } = \frac{{G{m^\prime }m}}{{{{\left( {\frac{{3R}}{2}} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{G\left( {\frac{M}{8}} \right)m}}{{\frac{{9{R^2}}}{4}}}$
$ = \frac{{GmM}}{{9{R^2}}} \times \frac{4}{8} = \frac{2}{9}F$ [From Eq (1)]
Thus, the force of attraction between the remaining part of the sphere and mass
$m=F-F^{\prime}=F-\dfrac{2}{9} F=\dfrac{7}{9} F$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Field Due to Hollow Sphere & Solid Sphere

question: The density of a solid sphere of radius $R$ is $\rho(r)=20 \dfrac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}$ where, $r$ is the distance from its centre. If the gravitational field due to this sphere at a distance $4 R$ from its centre is $E$ and $G$ is the gravitational constant, then the ratio of $E / G R$ is
explanation:
Volume of a spherical shell of thickness $d r$ and of radius $r$ is $d V=4 \pi r^{2} d r$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952622/preptracker/questions/q_109685_explanation_1764952622491.png So, mass of this shell is
$d M=\rho d V=\dfrac{20 r^{2}}{R^{2}} \cdot 4 \pi r^{2} \cdot d r$
Total mass of complete solid sphere is
$M=\int_{0}^{R} d M=\int_{0}^{R} \dfrac{80 \pi r^{4}}{R^{2}} d r$
$\Rightarrow M=\dfrac{80 \pi R^{5}}{5 R^{2}}=16 \pi R^{3}$
Due to this mass $M$, gravitational field intensity at a distance $4 R$ from its' centre is
$E=\dfrac{G M}{(4 R)^{2}} \Rightarrow \quad E=\dfrac{G \cdot 16 \pi R^{3}}{(4)^{2} R^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad E=G \pi R \Rightarrow \dfrac{E}{G R}=\pi$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Field Due to Hollow Sphere & Solid Sphere

question: The mass density inside a solid sphere of radius $r$ varies as $\rho(r)=\rho_{0}(r / R)^{\beta}$, where $\rho_{0}$ and $\beta$ are constants and $r$ is the distance from the centre. Let $E_{1}$ and $E_{2}$ be the gravitational fields due to the sphere at distance $R / 2$ and $2 R$ from the centre of sphere. If $\dfrac{E_{2}}{E_{1}}=4$, then the value of $\beta$ is
explanation:
Mass enclosed in a sphere of radius $r$ is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958057/preptracker/questions/q_109683_explanation_1764958056941.png $M_{r}=\int \rho d V=\int_{0}^{r} \rho_{0}\left(\dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{\beta} \cdot 4 \pi r^{2} d r$
$=\left[\dfrac{4 \pi \rho_{0}}{R^{\beta}} \cdot \dfrac{r^{\beta+3}}{\beta+3}\right]_{0}^{r}=\dfrac{4 \pi \rho_{0}}{R^{\beta}} \cdot \dfrac{r^{\beta+3}}{\beta+3}$
In a sphere of radius $\dfrac{R}{2}$,
$M_{1}=\dfrac{4 \pi \rho_{0}}{R^{\beta}} \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)^{\beta+3}}{(\beta+3)}=\dfrac{4 \pi \rho_{0} R^{3}}{(\beta+3)} \times \dfrac{1}{2^{\beta+3}}$
In a sphere of radius $R$ is
$M_{2}=\dfrac{4 \pi \rho_{0}}{R^{\beta}} \cdot \dfrac{R^{\beta+3}}{\beta+3}=\dfrac{4 \pi \rho_{0} R^{3}}{\beta+3}$
So, the gravitational field intensities are
$E_{1}=\dfrac{G M_{1}}{\left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{4 G}{R^{2}} \times \dfrac{4 \pi \rho_{0} R^{3}}{(\beta+3)} \times \dfrac{1}{2^{\beta+3}}$
and $E_{2}=\dfrac{G M_{2}}{(2 R)^{2}}=\dfrac{G}{4 R^{2}} \times \dfrac{4 \pi \rho_{0} R^{3}}{\beta+3}$
As $\dfrac{E_{2}}{E_{1}}=4$, we get
$\dfrac{\dfrac{G}{4 R^{2}} \times \dfrac{4 \pi \rho_{0} R^{3}}{\beta+3}}{\dfrac{4 G}{R^{2}} \times \dfrac{4 \pi \rho_{0} R^{3}}{\beta+3} \times \dfrac{1}{2^{\beta+3}}}=4$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{2^{\beta+3}}{16}=4 \Rightarrow 2^{\beta+3}=64$
$2^{\beta} \cdot 2^{3}=2^{6} \Rightarrow 2^{\beta}=8 \Rightarrow 2^{\beta}=2^{3} \Rightarrow \beta=3$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Field Due to Hollow Sphere & Solid Sphere

question: There are two bodies of masses 100 $kg$ and ${10,000 {~kg}}$ separated by a distance of 1 $m$ . At what distance from the smaller body, the intensity of gravitational field will be zero.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955932/preptracker/questions/q_36033_explanation_1764955932231.png Let the consider a point ${P}$ at a distance $x$ $m$ from the centre of smaller mass where intensity of gravitational field is zero.
Intensity due to ${m=}$ Intensity due to ${M}$$\begin{aligned}\therefore & \dfrac{G m}{x^{2}}=\dfrac{G M}{(1-x)^{2}} \\& \dfrac{100}{x^{2}}=\dfrac{10000}{(1-x)^{2}} \\& \left(\dfrac{10}{x}\right)= \pm\left(\dfrac{100}{1-x}\right) \\& 10(1-x)= \pm 100 x \\\Rightarrow & x=\dfrac{1}{11} \text { or } \dfrac{-1}{9}\end{aligned}$
Since distance cannot be negative
$\therefore x=\dfrac{1}{11} {~m}$.
So correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Field Due to a Point mass, Group of Point Masses

question: If a spherical shell is cut into two pieces along a chord as shown in figure and point $P$ and $Q$ have gravitational intensities $I_{P}$ and $I_{Q}$ respectively, then
explanation:
We know that inside a spherical shell gravitational field intensity at any point is zero, hence
$\begin{aligned}& I_{P}+I_{Q}=0 \\& \Rightarrow \quad I_{P}=I_{Q} \neq 0\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: At what distance (in metre) from the centre of the moon, the intensity of gravitational field will be zero? (Take, mass of earth and moon as $5.98 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{~kg}$ and $7.35 \times 10^{22} \mathrm{~kg}$ respectively and the distance between moon and earth is $3.855 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m}$.)
explanation:
Given, $M_{e}=5.98 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{~kg}$
${M_m} = 7.35 \times {10^{22}}\,\,kg{\text{ and}}$
$r = 3.855 \times {10^8}\,\,m$
Let $x$ be the distance of the point from the centre of earth where gravitational intensity is zero.
Therefore,
$\frac{{G{M_e}}}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{{G{M_m}}}{{{{\left( {3.85 \times {{10}^8} - x} \right)}^2}}}$
${\text{or }}\frac{x}{{3.85 \times {{10}^8} - x}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{M_e}}}{{{M_m}}}} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{5.98 \times {{10}^{24}}}}{{7.35 \times {{10}^{22}}}}} \cong 9$
${\text{or }}\quad \frac{x}{9} + x = 3.85 \times {10^8}$
$x = 9 \times 3.85 \times {10^8}/10$
$ = 3.46 \times {10^8}\,\,m$
Distance from moon
$ = 3.85 \times {10^8} - 3.46 \times {10^8}$
$ = 3.9 \times {10^7}\,\,m$
$ = 3.85 \times {10^7}\,\,m$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Field Due to a Point mass, Group of Point Masses

question: A solid sphere of mass $M$ and radius $R$ has a spherical cavity of radius $R / 2$ such that the centre of cavity is at a distance $R / 2$ from the centre of the sphere. A point mass $m$ is placed inside the cavity at a distance $R / 4$ from the centre of sphere. The gravitational pull between the sphere and the point mass $m$ is
explanation:
To calculate the force of attraction on the point mass $m$. We should calculate the force due to the solid sphere and subtract from this the force which the mass of the hallow sphere would have exerted on $m$, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951539/preptracker/questions/q_35905_explanation_1764951538577.png i.e. $F=\dfrac{G m M}{x^{2}}-\dfrac{G m M^{\prime}}{y^{2}}$
$[\because x=R / 4, x+y=R / 2]$
Since, mass,$M=\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right) \pi R^{3} \rho$
and $M^{\prime}=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi\left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)^{3} \rho=\dfrac{M}{8}$
$\therefore F=\dfrac{G M m}{(R / 4)^{2}}-\dfrac{G m(M / 8)}{(R / 4)^{2}}=\dfrac{14 G m M}{R^{2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Field Due to Hollow Sphere & Solid Sphere

question: Two bodies $A$ and $B$, having masses $m$ and $4 m$, are separated by distance $r$. Distance from $A$ at which gravitational field becomes zero is
explanation:
Let ${x}$ be the point where gravitational field is zero https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951596/preptracker/questions/q_35892_explanation_1764951596433.png ${\dfrac{G m}{x^{2}}=\dfrac{G(4 m)}{(r-x)^{2}}}$
$\dfrac{1}{x}=\dfrac{2}{r-x}$
$x=r / 3$

Assertion: If earth were a hollow sphere, gravitational field intensity at any point inside the earth would be zero.

Reason: Net force on a body inside the sphere is zero.
explanation: According to the shell theorem, if the Earth were hollow, the gravitational field $\vec{E}_{g}$ inside it would be zero. Force on mass $m$, $\vec{F}_{g}=\vec{E}_{g}=0$. So correct option is (1).

question: The magnitudes of gravitational field at distances $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ from the centre of a uniform sphere of radius $R$ and mass $M$ are $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$, respectively. Find the ratio $\left(I_{1} / I_{2}\right)$ if $r_{1}>R$ and $r_{2} < R$.
explanation:
The magnitude of gravitational field at a distance $r$ from the centre of a uniform sphere of radius $R$ and mass $M$ is as follows:
(i) For $r < R, I=\dfrac{G M}{R^{3}} r$
(ii) For $r>R, I=\dfrac{G M}{R^{2}}$
$\therefore I_{1}=\dfrac{G M}{r_{1}^{2}}$ and $I_{2}=\dfrac{G M r_{2}}{R^{3}}$
Their corresponding ratio is
$\frac{{{I_1}}}{{{I_2}}} = \frac{{GM}}{{r_1^2}} \times \frac{{{R^3}}}{{GM{r_2}}} = \frac{{{R^3}}}{{r_1^2{r_2}}}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Field Due to Hollow Sphere & Solid Sphere

question: The gravitational intensity in a region is $10(\hat i - \hat j)N{\rm{/}}kg$. The work done by the gravitational force to shift slowly a particle of mass $1\,kg$ from point $(1\,m,1\,m)$ to a point $(2\,m,{\rm{ }} - 2m)$ is
explanation:
$F_{g}=m \overrightarrow{E_{g}}=1(10(\hat{i}-\hat{j}))=10(\hat{i}-\hat{j}) N$
$=10 \hat{i}-10 \hat{j}$
$\vec{S}=(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j})-(\hat{i}-\hat{j})=\hat{i}-3 \hat{j} m$
Work done $=\overrightarrow{F_{g}} \cdot \vec{S}=(10 \hat{i}-10 \hat{j}) \cdot(\hat{i}-3 \hat{j})$
$ = 10(1) + ( - 10)( - 3) = 40\,J.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Field Due to a Point mass, Group of Point Masses

question: Gravitational field intensity at the centre of the semi circle formed by a thin wire $A B$ of mass ' $m$ ' and length ' $L$ ' is
explanation:
$\lambda=\dfrac{m}{L} ; L=\pi r ; d m=\lambda d l=\lambda(r d \theta)$
$\begin{aligned}& E=\dfrac{G \lambda}{r}\left[\int_{0}^{\pi} \cos \theta d \theta \hat{i}+\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin \theta d \theta \hat{j}\right] \\& \vec{E}=\dfrac{G \lambda}{r}\left[(\sin \theta)_{0}^{\pi} \hat{i}+(-\cos \theta)_{0}^{\pi} \hat{j}\right]\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}\Rightarrow \vec{E} & =\dfrac{2 G m}{r} \hat{j} \\& =\dfrac{2 G \lambda}{L \dfrac{L}{\pi}} \hat{j}\end{aligned}$
$=\dfrac{2 \pi G m}{L^{2}} \hat{j}$
subject: Physics

question: Two masses $90\,kg$ and $160\,kg$ are $5\,m$ apart. The gravitational field intensity at a point $3\,m$ from $90\,kg$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ from $160\,kg$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951436/preptracker/questions/q_35492_explanation_1764951436451.png $I = \sqrt {I_1^2 + I_2^2} $
$ = \sqrt {{{\left( {G\frac{{90}}{9}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {G\frac{{160}}{{16}}} \right)}^2}} $
$ = \sqrt {{{(10G)}^2} + {{(10G)}^2}} $
$ = 10\sqrt 2 G$
subject: Physics

question: Particles of masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ are at a fixed distance apart. If the gravitational field strength at $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ are $\vec{I}_{1}$ and $\vec{I}_{2}$ respectively. Then,
explanation:
$\vec{I}_{1}=\dfrac{G m_{2}}{d^{2}}$ and $\vec{I}_{2}=\dfrac{G m_{1}}{d^{2}}$
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2024_02_16_222f8193d082eec369b2g-2.jpg?height=122&width=274&top_left_y=2351&top_left_x=508
$\hat{n}$ is a unit vector directed from $m_{1}$ to $m_{2}$ along the line joining them. $\Rightarrow \vec{I}_{2}=-\dfrac{G m_{1}}{d^{2}} \hat{n}, \vec{I}_{1}=\dfrac{G m_{2}}{d^{2}} \hat{n}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{m_{1}} \vec{I}_{2}+\dfrac{1}{m_{2}} \vec{I}_{1}=0$
$\Rightarrow m_{1} \vec{I}_{1}+m_{2} \vec{I}_{2}=0$.
subject: Physics

question: A very long (length $L$) cylindrical galaxy is made of uniformly distributed mass and has radius $R(R <  < L)$. A star outside the galaxy is orbiting the galaxy in a plane perpendicular to the galaxy and passing through centre if the time period of star is $T$ and its distance from the galaxy's axis is $r$, then :
explanation:
The gravitational field varies as $\dfrac{1}{r}$ from the centre of the cylinder. The force on the star is
$F=\left(\dfrac{k}{r}\right) m$
$k$ is some constant
$\text { so } \begin{gathered}\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}=\dfrac{k m}{r} \Rightarrow v=\text { constant } \\T=\dfrac{2 \pi r}{v} \Rightarrow T \propto r\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Field Due to Infinitely Large Cylinder, Wire & Plane Surface

question: The gravitional field, due to 'left over part' of a uniform sphere (from which a part as shown, has been 'removed out'), at a very far off point, $P$, located as shown, would be (nearly) :
explanation:
Let mass of smaller sphere (which has to be removed ) is $m$
Radius $=\dfrac{R}{2}$ (from figure)
As densities are same
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{M}{\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}}=\dfrac{m}{\dfrac{4}{3} \pi\left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)^{3}} \\& \Rightarrow m=\dfrac{M}{8}\end{aligned}$
Mass of the left over part of the sphere
$M^{\prime}=M-\dfrac{M}{8}=\dfrac{7}{8} M$
A finite mass behaves like a point mass at large distances. The gravitational field due to the left over part of the sphere
$=\dfrac{7}{8} \dfrac{G M}{x^{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: A ring has mass $M$, radius $R$. A point mass $m$ is placed at a distance $x$ on the axial line as shown. Find $x$ so that force experienced is maximum.
explanation:
The force exerted by the ring on the point mass is $F=\dfrac{G M m x}{\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
When the force $F$ is maximum
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{d F}{d x}=G M m \dfrac{d}{d x}\left(\dfrac{x}{\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}\right)=0 \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{x \dfrac{d}{d x}\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}-\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2} \dfrac{d}{d x}(x)}{\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=0 \\& x \dfrac{3}{2}\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}(2 x)-\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}=0 \\& 3 x^{2}-\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)=0 \\& \Rightarrow \quad x=\dfrac{+R}{\sqrt{2}}\end{aligned}$

question: Figure shows a system of point masses placed on $X$-axis. Find the net gravitational field intensity at the origin.
explanation:
Net gravitational field intensity at the origin,
$E_{\text {net }}=E_{1}+E_{2}+E_{3}+\ldots$
$ = \frac{{GM}}{{{{(1)}^2}}}\widehat i + \frac{{GM}}{{{{(2)}^2}}}\widehat i + \frac{{GM}}{{{{(4)}^2}}}\widehat i + ...$
$ = GM\hat i\left[ {1 + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{{16}} + ......\infty } \right]$
$\left( {{\rm{Here}}\,a = 1\,{\rm{and}}\,r = \frac{1}{4}} \right)$
So, ${E_{net}} = GM\left( {\frac{a}{{1 - r}}} \right)\widehat i = GM\widehat i\left( {\frac{1}{{1 - \frac{1}{4}}}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,{E_{net}} = \frac{4}{3}GM\widehat i$

explanation:

question:
A spherical symmetric gravitational system of particles has a mass density
\(\rho=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc}\rho & \text { for } & r \leq R \\ 0 & \text { for } & r < R\end{array} \quad\right.\) where \(\rho_{0}\) is a constant. explanation:

$\frac{{m{v^2}}}{r} = \frac{{GM}}{{{R^3}}}r = \frac{G}{{{R^3}}}\left( {\rho \frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3}} \right)r$
$v\,\alpha \,r$

question: The energy required to take a body from the surface of the earth to a height equal to the radius of the earth is '$W$'. The energy required to take this body from the surface of the earth to a height equal to twice the radius of the earth is
explanation:
Work done $=U_{f}-U_{i}$
$\therefore W=-\dfrac{G M m}{2 R}-\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{R}\right)=\dfrac{G M m}{2 R}$
and $W^{\prime}=-\dfrac{G M m}{3 R}-\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{R}\right)=\dfrac{2 G M m}{3 R}$
$\therefore W^{\prime}=\dfrac{4}{3} W$

question: An object of mass '$m$' at a distance of '$20 R$' from the centre of a planet of mass '$M$' and radius '$R$' has an initial velocity '$u$'. The velocity with which the object hits the surface of the planet is ($G$ - Universal gravitational constant)
explanation:
Applying conservation of energy, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952635/preptracker/questions/q_109779_explanation_1764952634630.png $K_{i}+U_{i}=K_{f}+U_{f}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2}+\dfrac{-G M m}{20 R}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\dfrac{-G M m}{R}$
$\therefore v=\left(u^{2}+\dfrac{19 G M}{10 R}\right)^{1 / 2}$

question: Match the following columns related to gravitational potential energy and choose the correct option from the codes given below.
explanation:

B - P: The gravitational potential energy at a distance r from Earth's surface is given by $W(r)=G \dfrac{M_{E} m}{r}$, where $M_{E}$ is Earth's mass, $m$ is the mass of the particle.
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{R}$ : The potential energy difference between two heights $h_{1}$ and $h_{2}$ is given by${W_{12}} = W\left( {{h_1}} \right) - W\left( {{h_2}} \right)$, which is the change in gravitational potential energy.
D - S: The work done in lifting a particle from a radial distance $r_{1}$ to $r_{2}$ from the center of the Earth is $-G M_{E} m\left(\dfrac{1}{r_{2}}-\dfrac{1}{r_{1}}\right)$, according to the work-energy principle for conservative forces like gravity. Correct option is (1).

question: While a person climbs stairs, the gravitational potential energy of the person increases. The source of this energy is
explanation:
Potential energy increases by
$m g\left(h_{2}-h_{1}\right)=m g(h-0)=m g h$
This increase in potential energy is given by
$P E_{f}-P E_{t}=W$
where $W$ is the work done.
Thus, to increase the potential energy the work is done by internal forces inside the body.
So, the correct option is (4).

question: A body of mass $m$ is at a height $R$ from the surface of the earth where $R$ is the radius of the earth. If the body is taken from here to a height of $3 R$ from the surface of the earth, the increase in the gravitational potential energy of the body is ($g$ is acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the earth)
explanation:
At a height $h$ gravitational potential energy is given by
$U = - \frac{{GMm}}{{R + h}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Where $M=$ mass of earth.
Potential energy for $h=R$
${U_1} = - \frac{{GMm}}{{2R}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Potential energy for $h=3 R$
${U_2} = - \frac{{GMm}}{{4R}}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
From Eq. (3) and Eq. (2) increase in potential energy is
$U_{2}-U_{1}=\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{4 R}\right)-\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{2 R}\right)$
$\therefore \Delta U=\dfrac{G M m}{4 R}=\dfrac{m g R}{4}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Four particles each of mass $m$ are placed at four vertices of a rectangle having side length as $3 l_{0}$ and $4 l_{0}$. The magnitude of potential energy of the system in $\dfrac{G m^{2}}{l_{0}}$ is
explanation:
The given situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952614/preptracker/questions/q_109706_explanation_1764952613965.png $A C=B D=\sqrt{\left(4 l_{0}\right)^{2}+\left(3 l_{0}\right)^{2}}=5 l_{0}$
$\therefore$ Potential energy of the system,
$U=U_{A B}+U_{B C}+U_{C D}+U_{D A}+U_{A C}+U_{B D}$
Applying the formula, $U=-G \dfrac{m_{1} m_{2}}{r}$, we get
$U=-\dfrac{G \cdot m \cdot m}{4 l_{0}}-\dfrac{G \cdot m \cdot m}{3 l_{0}}-\dfrac{G \cdot m \cdot m}{4 l_{0}}$
$-\dfrac{G \cdot m \cdot m}{3 l_{0}}-\dfrac{G \cdot m \cdot m}{5 l_{0}}-\dfrac{G \cdot m \cdot m}{5 l_{0}}$
$=-\dfrac{G m^{2}}{l_{0}}\left[\dfrac{1}{4}+\dfrac{1}{3}+\dfrac{1}{4}+\dfrac{1}{3}+\dfrac{1}{5}+\dfrac{1}{5}\right]$
$ = - \frac{{G{m^2}}}{{{l_0}}}\left[ {\frac{1}{2} + \frac{2}{3} + \frac{2}{5}} \right]$
$ = - \frac{{G{m^2}}}{{{l_0}}}\left[ {\frac{{15 + 20 + 12}}{{30}}} \right]$
$=-\dfrac{G m^{2}}{l_{0}}\left(\dfrac{47}{30}\right)$
$|U|=\dfrac{47}{30}\left(\dfrac{G m^{2}}{l_{0}}\right)$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Four identical particles each of mass $m$ are kept at the corners of a square. If the gravitational force exerted on one of the particle by the other particles is $\left(\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2}+1}{32}\right) \dfrac{G m^{2}}{L^{2}}$, then the length of the side of the square is
explanation:
The situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958065/preptracker/questions/q_109703_explanation_1764958064849.png We know that, gravitational force,
$F=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{d^{2}}$
If we calculate net force on 2 , then
$F_{21}=F_{23}=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}}$
and $F_{24}=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{(a \sqrt{2})^{2}}=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{2 a^{2}}$
Now, as forces are vector quantity and angle between $F_{21}$ and $F_{23}$ is $90^{\circ}$.
$\therefore \quad F_{\text {net }}=F_{24}+\sqrt{F_{21}^{2}+F_{23}^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{2 a^{2}}+\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{2 a^{2}}+\sqrt{2\left(\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{2 a^{2}}+\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}} \sqrt{2}$
$=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}+\sqrt{2}\right)=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}}\left(\dfrac{1+2 \sqrt{2}}{2}\right)$
According to the question,
$F_{\text {net }}=\left(\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2}+1}{32}\right) \dfrac{G M^{2}}{L^{2}}$
Therefore, $a^{2}=16 L^{2} \Rightarrow a=4 L$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Match the following columns.
explanation:
The potential energy of satellite at distance $r$ from earth is, $U=\dfrac{-G M m}{r}$
So, it is always negative as it is due to attractive gravitation force of earth's field.
The kinetic energy of satellite is, $K=\dfrac{G M m}{2 r}$
It is always positive.
The total energy of satellite is
$T E=K+U=\dfrac{G M m}{2 r}-\dfrac{G M m}{r}=-\dfrac{G M m}{2 r}$
which is negative.
The gravitation potential energy of satellite at infinity is
$U=-\dfrac{G M m}{r}=-\dfrac{G M m}{\infty}=0$
So, the correct match is
A-Q, B-Q, C-P, D-R
So, the correct option is (3).

options.text: A - S, B - Q, C - P, D - R
A - R, B - P, C - S, D - Q
A - Q, B - Q, C - P, D - R
A - Q, B - P, C - S, D - R

subject: Physics

question: A mass $M$ is split into two parts $m_{0}$ and $M-m_{0}$. These two masses are then separated by a distance $D$. In order to make the gravitational force between the parts maximum, the ratio$\dfrac{m_{0}}{M}$ should be
explanation:
According to given situation, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952624/preptracker/questions/q_109692_explanation_1764952623932.png Again, $m_{0}$ and $M-m_{0}$ are kept at a distance $D$ gravitational force, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952625/preptracker/questions/q_109692_explanation_1764952624729.png $F=\dfrac{G m_{0}\left(M-m_{0}\right)}{D^{2}}$
$F$ will be maximum, when $\dfrac{d F}{d m_{0}}=0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d}{d m_{0}}\left(\dfrac{G m_{0}\left(M-m_{0}\right)}{D^{2}}\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d}{d m_{0}}\left(\dfrac{G m_{0} M-G m_{0}^{2}}{D^{2}}\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{G M-2 G m_{0}}{D^{2}}=0$
$\Rightarrow G M-2 G m_{0}=0 \Rightarrow M-2 m_{0}=0$
$\Rightarrow 2 m_{0}=M \Rightarrow \dfrac{m_{0}}{M}=\dfrac{1}{2}=0.5$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: From the pole of the earth, a body of mass $m$ is imparted a velocity $v_{0}$ directed vertically up. If $M$ is the mass of the earth, $R$ its radius and $g$ is the free-fall acceleration on its surface, then the height $h$ upto which the body will ascent is (neglect air resistance)
explanation:
By using conservation of energy, we get
$\Delta(K E)=\Delta(P E)$
$0-\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}=\dfrac{-G M m}{R}-\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{R+h}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{0}^{2}}{2 G m}=\dfrac{1}{R}-\dfrac{1}{R+h} \Rightarrow \dfrac{R(R+h)}{h}=\dfrac{2 g R^{2}}{v_{0}^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{R}{\left(\dfrac{2 g R}{v_{0}^{2}}-1\right)}$ or $h=\dfrac{R v_{0}^{2}}{2 g R-v_{0}^{2}}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: The gravitational potential at a point above the surface of earth is $ - 5.12 \times {10^7}\,J/kg$ and the acceleration due to gravity at that point is  $6.4\,m/{s^2}.$ Assume that the mean radius of earth to be  $6400\,km$ . The height of this point above the earth's surface is
explanation:
Gravitation potential at a point is given by
$V=\dfrac{-G M}{r}$ $\quad \quad (1)$
where $r$ is the distance between the point and centre of mass $M$ i.e., centre of earth
The acceleration due to gravity, $g=\dfrac{G M}{r^{2}}$ $\quad \quad (2)$
Dividing eqn. (1) and eqn. (2), we have
$\dfrac{V}{g}=\left(\dfrac{-G M}{r}\right)\left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{G M}\right)$
$\Rightarrow r=\dfrac{-V}{g}=\dfrac{-\left(-5.12 \times 10^{7}\right)}{6.4}$
$ = 8 \times {10^6}\,m = 8000\,km$
$\therefore$ Height of the point from earth's surface,
$h=r-R$
$\therefore h = 8000 - 6400 = 1600\,km$
So, correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Due to Circular Ring, Spherical Shell & Solid Sphere

question: In some region, the gravitational field is zero. The gravitational potential in this region
explanation:
$I = \frac{{dU}}{{dr}} = 0 \Rightarrow U$ is constant.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Gravitational Field and Potential

question:
The gravitational field in a region is given by
$\vec g = 5\,N{\rm{/}}kg\,\widehat i + 12\,N{\rm{/}}kg\,\widehat j$. The change in the gravitational potential energy of a particle of mass $1\,kg$ when it is taken from the origin to a point ($7\,m, - 3\,m$) is:
explanation:
$I = - \frac{{dv}}{{dr}}$
$v = - \left[ {\int_0^x {{I_x}} dx + \int_0^y {{I_y}} dy} \right] = - \left[ {{I_x} \cdot x + {I_y} \cdot y} \right]$
$ = - [35 + ( - 36)] = 1\,J{\rm{/}}kg$
$i.e.,$ change in gravitational potential $1\,J{\rm{/}}kg$. Hence change in gravitational potential energy $1\,J$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Three particles each of mass $m$ are present a $t$ the corners of an equilateral triangle of side $a$. Gravitational potential energy of the system is
explanation:
$U=-\dfrac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r}$
Therefore, for three particle system,
$U=-\dfrac{3 G m^{2}}{a}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: If the gravitational field in the space is given as $\left(-\dfrac{K}{r^{2}}\right)$. Taking the reference point to be at $r = 2\,cm$ with gravitational potential $V = 10\,J{\rm{/}}kg$. Find the gravitational potential at $r = 3\,cm$ in SI unit (Given, that $K = 6\,J\,cm/kg$)
explanation:
We know, $g=\left(\dfrac{-K}{r^{2}}\right)$
Relation between gravitational field and gravitational potential is given by
$\dfrac{-d V}{d r}=E_{g}$ or $g$
or $\dfrac{-d V}{d r}=\dfrac{-K}{r^{2}}$ or $\int_{V_{i}}^{V_{f}} d V=K \int_{2}^{3} \dfrac{1}{r^{2}} d r$
or $\left[V_{3}-V_{2}\right]=-K\left[\dfrac{1}{3}-\dfrac{1}{2}\right]$
or $V_{3}-10=-6\left[\dfrac{2-3}{6}\right]$
or ${V_3} = 1 + 10 = 11\,V$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Gravitational Field and Potential

question: A body of mass is taken from earth surface to the height $h$ equal to twice the radius of earth $\left(R_{e}\right)$, the increase in potential energy will be ($g=$ acceleration due to gravity on the surface of Earth)
explanation:
The potential energy of a system of two masses is given by, $\quad U=-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{r}$
Potential energy on earth's surface
${U_1} = - \frac{{G{M_e}m}}{R}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Potential energy at height $h=2 R$ is
${U_2} = \frac{{ - G{M_e}m}}{{(2R + R)}} = \frac{{ - G{M_e}m}}{{3R}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Increase in $P . E$
$\Delta U=U_{2}-U_{1}=-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{3 R_{e}}+\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{R_{e}}$
$\Delta U=\dfrac{2}{3} \dfrac{G M_{e} m}{R_{e}}$
As $g=\dfrac{G M_{e}}{R_{e}^{2}}$
$\Delta U=\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{g R_{e}^{2} m}{R_{e}} ; \Delta U=\dfrac{2}{3} m g R_{e}$
So, correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A body is released from a height equal to the radius $(R)$ of the earth. The velocity of the body when it strikes the surface of the earth will be (Given $g=$ acceleration due to gravity on the earth)
explanation:
Applying law of conservation of energy,Total energy of body (K.E. + P.E.) on the earth's surface $=$ Total energy of the body at a height $h$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951569/preptracker/questions/q_35776_explanation_1764951569497.png $T E_{A}=T E_{B}$
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\left(\dfrac{-G M m}{R}\right)=\dfrac{-G M m}{R+h}+\dfrac{1}{2} m(0)^{2}$
or given $\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\dfrac{G M m}{R}=\dfrac{-G M m}{2 R}$
$(\because h=R($ given $)$)
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{G M m}{2 R}$
$\text { or } v=\sqrt{\dfrac{G M}{R}} \text { or } v=\sqrt{\dfrac{G M R}{R^{2}}}$
We know that, $g=\dfrac{G M}{R^{2}}$
$\therefore v=\sqrt{g R}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: If $V$ is the gravitational potential due to sphere of uniform density on it's surface, then its value at the centre of sphere will be
explanation:
Gravitation potential at a point inside the sphere is given by
$V_{\text {lnside }}=\dfrac{-G M}{2 R^{3}}\left(3 R^{2}-x^{2}\right)$
Where $x$ is distance from centre of sphere
$V_{\text {inside }}=-G M\left(\dfrac{3}{2 R}-\dfrac{r^{2}}{2 R^{3}}\right) \quad($ if $x=r)$
At surface of sphere, $V_{\text {surface }}=\dfrac{-G M}{x}$; if $(x=R)$
At centre of sphere, $x=0$
$\Rightarrow V_{c}=-\dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{G M}{R}=\dfrac{3}{2} V_{\text {surface }}=\dfrac{3}{2} V$
So, answer is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Due to Circular Ring, Spherical Shell & Solid Sphere

question: Two rings having masses $M$ and $2 M$, respectively, having same radius are placed coaxially as shown in figure. If the mass distribution on both the rings is non-uniform, then gravitational potential at point $P$ is
explanation:
As all the points on the periphery of either ring are at same distance from point $P$ the potential at point $P$ due to whole ring can be calculated as $V=-(G M)\left(\sqrt{R^{2}+x^{2}}\right)$ where $x$ is axial distance from the centre of the ring. This expression is independent of the fact whether the distribution of mass is uniform are nonuniform.
$\begin{aligned}& V \text { at } P \text { is } V=-\dfrac{G M}{\sqrt{2 R}}-\dfrac{G 2 M}{\sqrt{5 R}} \\& =-\dfrac{G M}{R}\left[\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right] .\end{aligned}$

options.text: Cannot be determined from given information.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Due to Circular Ring, Spherical Shell & Solid Sphere

question: Two bodies of masses $m$ and $M$ are placed a distance $d$ apart. The gravitational potential at the position where the gravitational field due to them is zero is
explanation:
If net gravitation field of $p$ becomes zero means
$\dfrac{G M}{x^{2}}=\dfrac{G M}{(d-x)^{2}} \Rightarrow x=\dfrac{(\sqrt{m}) d}{\sqrt{m}+\sqrt{M}}$ and
$d-x=\dfrac{(\sqrt{M}) d}{\sqrt{m}+\sqrt{M}}$
Gravitational potential at
$\dfrac{-G M}{\dfrac{(\sqrt{m}) d}{\sqrt{m}+\sqrt{M}}}+\dfrac{-G M}{\dfrac{(\sqrt{M}) d}{\sqrt{m}+\sqrt{M}}}=-\dfrac{G}{d}(\sqrt{m}+\sqrt{M})^{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Due to a Point Mass/Group of Point Masses

question: The work done in slowly lifting a body from earth's surface to a height $R$ (radius of earth) is equal to two times the work done in lifting the same body from earth's surface to a height $h$. Here $h$ is equal to
explanation:
Work done = increase in gravitational potential energy
$W_{R \rightarrow 2 R}=2\left(W_{R \rightarrow R+h}\right)$
$\left(U_{f}-U_{i}\right)=2\left(U_{f}-U_{i}\right)$
$-\dfrac{G M m}{R+R}+\dfrac{G M m}{R}=2\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{R+h}+\dfrac{G M m}{R}\right)$
$\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{R}{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: \(P(r)\) is magnitude of physical quantity as a function of \(r\) (distance from centre of spherical distribution of radius \(R\) ). Match the following columns and select the correct option from the codes given below:
explanation:
(A - Q) $P(r)$ shows a constant value until $r=R$ and then drops off. This represents the variation of gravitational potential $P$ for a uniform spherical shell.
$(\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{R}) P(r)$ shows a linear increase with $r$ until $r=R$ and then drops to zero. This represents the variation of gravitational field intensity for a uniform solid sphere
(C - S) $P(r)$ shows a spike at $r=R$. This represents the variation of gravitational field intensity $P$ for a uniform spherical shell. The gravitational field is zero inside a spherical shell and has a spike at the surface due to the mass distribution.
(D - P) $P(r)$ shows an increase until $r=R$ and then gravitational potential decreases. Correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Due to Circular Ring, Spherical Shell & Solid Sphere

question: The gravitational field in a region is given by $(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}) N / k g$. What is the work done by an external agent to slowly shift a particle of mass $10\,kg$ from the point $(0,0)$ to a point $(5\,m,4\,m)$?
explanation:
The work done by external agent is
$W=m \Delta V=m(\vec{E} . \Delta \vec{r})$
The displacement is
$\Delta \vec r = 5\hat i + 4\hat j$
$W = 10(2\hat i + 2\hat j) \cdot (5\hat i + 4\hat j)$
$ = 10(10 + 8) = 180\,J.$
subject: Physics

question: The magnitude of $P E$ of three objects of masses $1\,kg,2\,kg$ and $3\,kg$ placed at the three vertices of an equilateral triangle of side $20\,cm$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955901/preptracker/questions/q_35469_explanation_1764955901128.png $U = \frac{G}{a}[2 + 6 + 3]$
$ \Rightarrow U = - \frac{{G(11)}}{a}$
$ = - \frac{{G(11)}}{{20}} \times 100 = - 55\,G$
$ \Rightarrow |U| = 55\,G$.

question: What is the maximum height attained by a body projected with a velocity equal to one-third of the escape velocity from the surface of the earth? (Radius of earth $=R$ )
explanation:
From conservation of energy,
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}-\dfrac{G M m}{R}=\dfrac{-G M m}{R+h} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m \dfrac{1}{9} \dfrac{2 G M}{R}=\dfrac{G M m h}{R+H}\end{aligned}$
$9 h=R+h \Rightarrow h=R / 8$

question: An asteroid of mass $m$ is approaching earth, initially at a distance of $10 R_{e}$, with speed $v_{i}$. It hits the earth with a speed $v_{f}\left(R_{e}\right.$ and $M_{e}$ are radius and mass of earth), then
explanation:
Applying law of conservation of energy for asteroid at a distance $10 R_{e}$ and at earth's surface,
${\left( {{E_K}} \right)_i} + {U_i} = {\left( {{E_K}} \right)_f} + {U_f}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951507/preptracker/questions/q_35381_explanation_1764951506756.png Now, $\left(E_{K}\right)_{i}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{i}^{2}$ and $U_{i}=-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{10 R_{e}}$
$\left(E_{K}\right)_{f}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{f}^{2}$ and $U_{f}=-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{R_{e}}$
On substituting these values in Eq. (1), we get $\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{i}^{2}-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{10 R_{e}}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{f}^{2}-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{R_{e}}$
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{f}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{i}^{2}+\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{R_{e}}-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{10 R_{e}}$
$\Rightarrow v_{f}^{2}=v_{i}^{2}+\dfrac{2 G M_{e}}{R_{e}}-\dfrac{2 G M_{e}}{10 R_{e}}$$\therefore v_{f}^{2}=v_{i}^{2}+\dfrac{2 G M_{e}}{R_{e}}\left(1-\dfrac{1}{10}\right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Two bodies of mass $m$ and $9 m$ are placed at a distance $R$. The gravitational potential on the line joining the bodies where the gravitational field equals zero, will be ( $G=$ gravitational constant):
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951479/preptracker/questions/q_35353_explanation_1764951478934.png Gravitational field is zero at point $'P'$.
$\frac{{Gm}}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{{G\,\,9\,m}}{{{{(R - x)}^2}}}$
$\frac{{R - x}}{x} = \sqrt 9 $
$x = \frac{R}{4}$
Gravitational potential at a point $'P'$.
${V_P} = {V_1} + {V_2}$
${V_P} = - \frac{{Gm}}{{\frac{R}{4}}} - \frac{{G(9\,m)}}{{\frac{{3\,R}}{4}}}$
${V_P} = - \frac{{Gm}}{R}[4 + 12]$
${V_P} = - \frac{{Gm}}{R}(16)$
Correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following most closely depicts the correct variation of the gravitational potential \[V(r)\] due to a large planet of radius \[R\] and uniform mass density? (Figures are not drawn to scale)

question:
The gravitational field in a region is given by:
$\vec E = (5\,N/kg)\widehat i + (12\,N/kg)\widehat j$
If the potential at the origin is taken to be zero, Then the ratio of the potential at the points $(12\,m,\,0)$ and $(0.5\,m)$ is :
explanation:
From question,
${E_x} = 5N/kg\,$ and ${E_y} = 12N/kg$
$V = Ed$
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {V_{(12\,\,m,0)}} = {E_x} \times 12\,J/kg$
and ${V_{(0,5\,\,m)}} = {E_y} \times 5\,J/kg$
(Given : potential at the origin is zero)
$\therefore \frac{{{V_{(12\,\,m,0)}}}}{{{V_{(0,2\,\,m)}}}} = \frac{{{E_x} \times 12}}{{{E_y} \times 5}} = \frac{{5 \times 12}}{{12 \times 5}} = 1$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Gravitational Field and Potential

question: A tunnel made is along a chord of the earth at a perpendicular distance \[R/2\] from the earth's centre. The wall of the tunnel is assumed to be frictionless. A particle is released from one end of the tunnel. The pressing force by the particle on the wall and the acceleration of the particle varies with \(x\) (distance of the particle from the centre) accroding to:
explanation:
The force on a particle at a distance $x$ From the centre is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951418/preptracker/questions/q_35229_explanation_1764951417590.png $F = \frac{{GMm}}{{{R^3}}}x$ ($M$ - Mass of the earth)
$F = \frac{{GMm}}{{{R^3}}}x$ ($M$- Mass of the earth)
The component of force perpendicular to the tunnel is $F \sin \theta=\dfrac{G M m}{2 R^{2}}$.

question: The gravitational field in a region is given by $I = (4\hat i + \hat j)Nk{g^{ - 1}}$. Work done by this field is zero when a particle is moved along the line
explanation:
Work done by the gravitational field is zero, when displacement is perpendicular to gravitational field. Here, gravitational field, $I=4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}$. If $\theta_{1}$ is the angle which field makes with positive $x$-axis, then
$\tan \theta_{1}=\dfrac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \theta_{1}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)=14^{\circ} 6^{\prime} \text { (Clockwise). }$
If $\theta_{2}$ is the angle which the line $y+4 x=6$ makes with positive $x$-axis, then $\theta_{2}=\tan ^{-1}(-4)=-75^{\circ} 56^{\prime}$ (Anti clockwise)
so $\left|\theta_{1}\right|+\left|\theta_{2}\right|=90^{\circ}$
i.e., the line $y+4 x=6$ is perpendicular to $\boldsymbol{I}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Let the minimum external work done in shifting a particle of mass $m$ from centre of earth to earth's surface be $W_{1}$ and that from surface of earth to inifinity be $W_{2}$. Then $\dfrac{W_{1}}{W_{2}}$ is equal to
explanation:
$W_{1}=U_{\text {Surface }}-U_{\text {centre }}$
$\begin{aligned}& W_{2}=U_{\infty}-U_{\text {Surface }} \\& \dfrac{W_{1}}{W_{2}}=\dfrac{-\dfrac{G M}{R}-\left(-\dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{G M}{R}\right)}{0-\left(-\dfrac{G M}{R}\right)}=\dfrac{1}{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question:
A particle is projected from point $A$, that is at a distance 4$R$ from the centre of the Earth, with speed $v_{1}$ in a direction making $30^{\circ}$ with the line joining the centre of the Earth and point $A$, as shown. Find the speed $v_{1}$ of particle (in $m/s$) if particle passes grazing the surface of the earth. Consider gravitational interaction only between earth and the particle.
(use $\frac{{GM}}{R} = 6.4 \times {10^7}\,{m^2}/{s^2}$)
explanation:
Conserving angular momentum of the particle w.r.to the centre of the earth.
$m\left(v_{1} \cos 60\right) 4 R=m v_{2} R \Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{2}}{v_{1}}=2 .$
Conserving energy of the system:
$\begin{aligned}& -\dfrac{G M m}{4 R}+\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}=-\dfrac{G M m}{R}+\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{2}^{2} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} v_{2}^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} v_{1}^{2}=\dfrac{3}{4} \dfrac{G M}{R} \Rightarrow v_{1}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{G M}{R} \\& \Rightarrow v_{1}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{64 \times 10^{6}}=\dfrac{8000}{\sqrt{2}} m / s\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: The potential energy of gravitational interaction of a point mass $m$ and a thin uniform rod of mass $M$ and length $l$, if they are located along a straight line at distance $a$ (From the nearest end of the rod) from each other is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955850/preptracker/questions/q_35173_explanation_1764955850376.png Mass of small element of length, $d M=\dfrac{M}{l} d x$
The gravitational potential energy between this element and point mass is
$dU = \frac{{ - GmdM}}{x} = - \frac{{Gm\left( {\frac{M}{l}dx} \right)}}{x}$
$\therefore U = - \frac{{GMm}}{l}\int\limits_a^{a + \ell } {\frac{{dx}}{x}} $
$U = - \frac{{GMm}}{l}1n\left( {\frac{{a + \ell }}{a}} \right)$

question: A body of mass $m$ $kg$ starts falling from a point 2$R$ above the Earth's surface. Its kinetic energy when it has fallen to a point ' $R$ ' above the Earth's surface [$R$-Radius of Earth, $M$-Mass of Earth, $G$-Gravitational Constant]
explanation:
Gravitational Potential energy
$\begin{aligned}& U=\dfrac{-G M m}{r}=\dfrac{-G M m}{R+h} \\& U_{\text {initial }}=-\dfrac{G M m}{3 R} \text { and } U_{\text {final }}=\dfrac{-G M m}{2 R}\end{aligned}$
Loss in $P E=$ gain in $KE$ $=\dfrac{G M m}{2 R}-\dfrac{G M m}{3 R}=\dfrac{G M m}{6 R}$

question: Three identical masses $m$ each are kept at the corners of an equilateral triangle of side $a$. The work done by external agent to increase the side of the triangle from $a$ to $2 a$, is
explanation:

question: A rocket of mass $M$ is launched vertically from the surface of the earth with an initial speed $V$. Assuming the radius of the earth to be $R$ and negligible air resistance, the maximum height attained by the rocket above the surface of the earth is
explanation:
From conservation of
$ energy\,\frac{1}{2}m{V^2} = G{M_e}m\left( {\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{{R + h}}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\operatorname{Also} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} g = \frac{{G{M_e}}}{{{R^2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (2)$
On solving (1) and (2)
$h=\dfrac{V^{2} R^{2}}{2 G M-V^{2} R}=\dfrac{R}{\left(\dfrac{2 g R}{V^{2}}-1\right)}$

question: A hollow spherical shell is compressed to half of its radius. The gravitational potential at the centre
explanation:

question:
If $W_{1}, W_{2}$ and $W_{3}$ represent the work done in moving a particle from $A$ and $B$ along three different paths 1, 2 and 3, respectively (As shown in the figure) in the gravitational field of a point mass $m$, find the correct relation
between $W_{1}, W_{2}$ and $W_{3}$.
explanation:
As gravitational force is a conservative force, work done is independent of path.
$W_{1}=W_{2}=W_{3}$
