question:
From a solid sphere of mass $M$ and radius $R$, a spherical portion of radius $\dfrac{R}{2}$ is removed, as shown in the figure. Taking gravitational potential $V=0$ at $r=\infty$, the potential at the centre of the cavity thus formed is
($G=$ Gravitational constant)
explanation:
The sphere with cavity can be assumed as a combination of two spheres. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951342/preptracker/questions/q_34777_explanation_1764951342201.png Potential at internal point of solid sphere at a distance $'r'$
$V=-\dfrac{G M}{R}\left[\dfrac{3}{2}-\dfrac{r^{2}}{2 R^{2}}\right]$
At $r = \frac{R}{2}$
${V_1} = \frac{{ - GM}}{R}\left[ {\frac{3}{2} - \frac{{{R^2}}}{{8{R^2}}}} \right] = - \frac{{11}}{8}\frac{{GM}}{R}$
Potential by the removed sphere is
$V_{2}=\dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{G\left(\dfrac{-M}{8}\right)}{\dfrac{R}{2}}=\dfrac{3}{8} \dfrac{G M}{R}(\text { Sphere mass is }(-) v e)$
Net potential, $V=V_{1}+V_{2}=-\dfrac{11}{8} \dfrac{G M}{R}+\dfrac{3}{8} \dfrac{G M}{R}$
$=-\dfrac{G M}{R}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Due to Circular Ring, Spherical Shell & Solid Sphere

question: Taking the gravitational potential at a point infinite distance away as zero, the gravitational potential at a point $A$ is $ - 5$ unit. If the gravitational potential at point infinite distance away is taken as +10 units, the potential at point $A$ is
explanation:
When reference point potential is changed then potential difference between any two points remains constant.
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta V_{\text {Old }}=\Delta V_{\text {New }} \\& \left(V_{\infty}-V_{P}\right)_{\text {Old }}=\left(V_{\infty}-V_{P}\right)_{\text {New }} \\& 0-(-5)=\left(10-V_{P_{\text {New }}}\right) \Rightarrow V_{P_{\text {New }}}=5 \text { unit }\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Due to a Point Mass/Group of Point Masses

question: Two identical thin rings each of radius $R$ are coaxially placed at a distance $R$. If the rings have a uniform mass distribution and each has mass $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ respectively, then the work done in moving a mass $m$ from centre of one ring to that of the other is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955787/preptracker/questions/q_34749_explanation_1764955786740.png $V_{A}=\left(\right.$ Potential at $A$ due to $\left.m_{1}\right)+($ Potential at $A$ due to $m_{2}$ )
$\Rightarrow V_{A}=-\dfrac{G m_{1}}{R}-\dfrac{G m_{2}}{\sqrt{2} R}$
Similarly,
$V_{B}=\left(\right.$ Potential at $B$ due to $\left.m_{1}\right)+($ Potential at $B$ due to $m_{2}$ )
$\Rightarrow V_{B}=-\dfrac{G m_{2}}{R}-\dfrac{G m_{1}}{\sqrt{2} R}$
Since, $W_{A \rightarrow B}=m\left(V_{B}-V_{A}\right)$
$=\dfrac{G m\left(m_{1}-m_{2}\right)(\sqrt{2}-1)}{\sqrt{2} R}$
$U=-\dfrac{G M M}{R}=-\dfrac{G M^{2}}{R}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: The self gravitational potential energy of a spherical shell of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is
explanation:
Assume that the total mass $M$ is present at infinity. We bring the total mass and form like a sphere. Let $m$ is the mass of the shell having radius $R$. To bring further mass $dm$ from infinity the work done by the external agent is
$d{W_{{\text{ext }}}} = dU = {U_f} - {U_i} = {U_R} - {U_\infty }$
$d{W_{{\text{ext }}}} = \left( {\frac{{ - Gm}}{R}{\text{d m}} - \frac{{Gmdm}}{\infty }} \right)$
${W_{{\text{ext }}}} = \frac{{ - G}}{R}\int_0^M m dm = \frac{{ - G{M^2}}}{{2R}}$
${W_{{\text{ext }}}} = {U_{{\text{Self }}}} = \frac{{ - G{M^2}}}{{2R}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Self Energy of Spherical Bodies

explanation:
The potential energy of the system
$U = - \frac{{GMM}}{R} = - \frac{{G{M^2}}}{R}$

question: Distance between the centres of two stars is $10 a$. The masses of these stars are $M$ and 16$M$ and their radii $a$ and $2 a$ respectively. A body of mass $m$ is fired straight from the surface of the largger star towards the smaller star. The minimum initial speed for the body to reach the surface of smaller star is
explanation:
First we find a point where the resultant field due to both is zero. Let the point $P$ be at a distance $x$ from centre of bigger star. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956918/preptracker/questions/q_34738_explanation_1764956918043.png $\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{G(16 M)}{x^{2}}=\dfrac{G M}{(10 a-x)^{2}} \\& \Rightarrow \quad x=8 a \quad\left(\text { from } O_{1}\right)\end{aligned}$
i.e., once the body reaches $P$, the gravitational pull of attraction due to $M$ takes the lead to make $m$ move towards it automatically. A minimum $KE$ has to be imparted to$m$ from surface of 16$M$ such that it is just able to reach point $P$. By law of conservation of energy.
(Total mechanical energy at $A$)
$=($ Total mechanical energy at $P$)
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v_{\min }^{2}+\left[\dfrac{-G(16 M) m}{2 a}-\dfrac{G(M) m}{8 a}\right] \\& =0+\left[\dfrac{-G M m}{2 a}-\dfrac{G(16 M) m}{8 a}\right] \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v_{\min }^{2}=\dfrac{G M m}{8 a}(45)\end{aligned}$

$\Rightarrow \quad v_{\min }=\dfrac{3}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{5 G M}{a}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: The gravitational field in a region is given by $\vec g = 5\,N/kg\hat i + 12\,N/kg\hat j$. The change in the gravitational potential energy of a particle of mass 1 $kg$ when it is taken from the origin to a point $(7 m,-3 m)$ is:
explanation:
Gravitational field, $\overrightarrow g = \left( {5\hat i + 12\hat j} \right)N/kg$
$g = - \frac{{dV}}{{dr}}$
$V = - \left[ {\int\limits_0^x {{g_x}dx + \int\limits_0^y {{g_x}dy} } } \right] = - \left[ {{g_x}.x + {g_x}.y} \right]$
i.e., change in gravitational potential 1$J$/$kg$.
Hence change in gravitational potential energy 1 $J$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Infinite number of bodies, each of mass 4 $kg$, are situated on $x$-axis at distance $1\,m,2\,m,4\,m,8\,m$, respectively, from the origin. The resulting gravitational potential due to this system at the origin will be:
explanation:
The potential at the origin is
$V=-4 G\left[\dfrac{1}{1}+\dfrac{1}{2}+\dfrac{1}{4}+\dfrac{1}{8} \ldots\right]$
$=-4 G\left[1+\dfrac{1}{2}+\dfrac{1}{2^{2}}+\dfrac{1}{2^{3}} \cdots\right]$
$=-2 G\left[\dfrac{a}{1-r}\right]$
where$a = 1\,\& \,{\text{r = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}$
$=-4 G \dfrac{1}{\left(1-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)}=-8 G$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Due to a Point Mass/Group of Point Masses

question: Given that mass of the earth is $M$ and its radius is $R$. A body is dropped from a height equal to the radius of the earth. When it reaches the ground its velocity will be :-
explanation:
From conservation of energy
$\begin{aligned}& T \cdot E_{i}=T \cdot E_{f} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{-G M m}{2 R}+0=\dfrac{-G M m}{R}+\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \\& \Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\dfrac{G M}{R}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Two bodies of massess $M$ and $4 M$ are placed at a distance $r$. The gravitational potential at a point on the line joining, where the gravitional field is zero, is
explanation:
Let gravitational field is zero at a distance $x$ from the mass $M$.
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{G M}{x^{2}}=\dfrac{G(4 M)}{(r-x)^{2}} \\& \Rightarrow 4 x^{2}=(r-x)^{2} \\& 2 x=r-x \Rightarrow x=\dfrac{r}{3}\end{aligned}$
The potential at that point is
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore V_{p}=-\dfrac{G M}{x}-\dfrac{G(4 M)}{r-x} \\& =-\dfrac{3 G M}{r}-\dfrac{6 G M}{r}=-\dfrac{9 G M}{r}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Relation between Gravitational Field and Potential

question: Four particles each of mass $m$ are placed at the vertices of a square of side $l$. The potential energy of the system is
explanation:
The four particles are shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951292/preptracker/questions/q_34624_explanation_1764951291701.png From figure
$\begin{aligned}& A B=B C=C D=A D=l \\& \therefore A C=B D=\sqrt{l^{2}+l^{2}}=l \sqrt{2}\end{aligned}$
Total potential energy of the system of four particles each of mass $m$ placed at the vertices $A, B, C$ and $D$ of a square is
$\begin{aligned}& U=\left(\dfrac{G \times m \times m}{A B}\right)+\left(-\dfrac{G \times m \times m}{A C}\right) \\& +\left(-\dfrac{G \times m \times m}{A D}\right)+\left(-\dfrac{G \times m \times m}{B C}\right) \\& +\left(-\dfrac{G \times m \times m}{B D}\right)+\left(-\dfrac{G \times m \times m}{C D}\right) \\& =-\dfrac{4 G m^{2}}{l}-\dfrac{2 G m^{2}}{l \sqrt{2}}=-\dfrac{2 G m^{2}}{l}\left(2+\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Two bodies of masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ are initially at rest at infinite distance apart. They are then allowed to move towards each other under mutual gravitational attraction. Their relative velocity of approach at a separation of $r$ between them is
explanation:
Apply the principle of conservation of linear momentum and conservation of energy.
Let velocities of these bodies at $r$ distance from each other be $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ respectively.
By conservation of momentum
${m_1}{v_1} - {m_2}{v_2} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow {m_1}{v_1} = {m_2}{v_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
From conservation of energy
$K{E_i} + {U_i} = K{E_f} + {U_f}$
$0 = \frac{{ - G{m_1}{m_2}}}{r} + KE$
$\frac{{G{m_1}{m_2}}}{r} = \frac{1}{2}{m_1}v_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}{m_2}v_2^2\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) & (2)
$\begin{gathered}v_{1}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G m_{2}^{2}}{r\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)}}, v_{2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G m_{1}^{2}}{r\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)}} \\\therefore v_{\text {rel }}=\left|v_{1}\right|+\left|v_{2}\right|=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G}{r}\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A particle of mass ' $m$ ' is projected with a velocity $u = k{v_e}(k < 1)$ from the surface of the earth. $\left( {{v_e}} \right. = $ escape velocity) The maximum height above the surface reached by the particle is
explanation:
Let $h$ be the maximum height reached by the particle.
From conservation of energy
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{-G M m}{h}+\dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2}=-\dfrac{G M m}{R+h} \\& -\dfrac{G M m^{2}}{h}=\dfrac{G M m}{h}+\dfrac{1}{2} m k^{2} \dfrac{2 G M}{R} \\& \dfrac{-1}{R}+\dfrac{k^{2}}{R}=\dfrac{-1}{R+h} \\& h=\dfrac{R k^{2}}{1-k^{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

Since weight $W$ is also force

question: A body is thrown from the surface of the earth with velocity $u\,m/s$. The maximum height in metre above the surface of the earth up to which it will reach is ( where $R=$ radius of earth, $g=$ acceleration due to gravity)
explanation:
Given, velocity of the body $ = u\,m/s$
Using conservation of energy between surface and maximum height,
${U_1} + {K_1} = {U_2} + {K_2}$
$-\dfrac{G M m}{R}+\dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2}=\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{R+h}\right)+0$
$\dfrac{G M}{R+h}=\dfrac{G M}{R}-\dfrac{u^{2}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{R+h}{G M}=\dfrac{2 R}{2 G M-R u^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{2 G M R}{2 G M-R u^{2}}-R$
$ \Rightarrow h = \frac{{{R^2}{u^2}}}{{2GM - R{u^2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
But $g=\dfrac{G M}{R^{2}}$
$ \Rightarrow GM = g{R^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Substituting the value of eq.(2) in eq.(1), we get
$h=\dfrac{R u^{2}}{2 g R-u^{2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Two particles of masses $m$ and $9 m$ are separated by a distance $r$. At a point on the line joining them the gravitational field is zero. The gravitational potential at that point is $(G=$ universal constant of gravitation)
explanation:
Let the gravitational field be zero at a point $'P'$ located on the line joining the two masses, at a distance $x$ from the smaller mass.
The magnitudes of gravitational field intensities by the two masses are equal at $'P'$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{G m}{x^{2}}=\dfrac{G(9 m)}{(r-x)^{2}} \\& \Rightarrow 9 x^{2}-(r-x)^{2}=0 \Rightarrow(2 x+r)(4 x-r)=0\end{aligned}$
Neglecting the negative root,
$\Rightarrow x=\dfrac{r}{4}$
Gravitational potential at this point is given by
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}=-\left\{\dfrac{G m}{\dfrac{r}{4}}+\dfrac{G(9 m)}{\dfrac{3 r}{4}}\right\}\left[\because r-x=\dfrac{3 r}{4}\right] \\& =-\left(\dfrac{4 G m+12 G m}{r}\right)=-\left(\dfrac{16 G m}{r}\right)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Due to a Point Mass/Group of Point Masses

question: A body of mass $m$ is kept at a small height $h$ above the ground. If the radius of the earth is $R$ and its mass is $M$, the potential energy of the body and earth system (with $h=\infty$ being the reference position) is
explanation:
On the surface of earth $U=-\dfrac{G M m}{R}$
At height $h( < < R)$, increase in potential energy is $mgh$
$\therefore {U_h} = - \frac{{GMm}}{R} + mgh$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A body is thrown from the surface of the earth with velocity $v\,m/s$. The maximum height above the earth's surface up to which it will reach is ($R=$ radius of earth, $g=$ acceleration due to gravity)
explanation:
Let the maximum height where the velocity of body becomes zero be $h$.
Applying principle of conservation of energy, we get
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\dfrac{G M m}{R}=\dfrac{1}{2} m(0)^{2}-\dfrac{G M m}{(R+h)}$
[ where $m=$ mass of body and $M=$ mass of earth]
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=G M m\left(\dfrac{1}{R}-\dfrac{1}{R+h}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{h}{R(R+h)}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{2 G M}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{h}{R(R+h)}=\dfrac{v^{2} R}{2 g R^{2}}\left(\because G M=g R^{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{R+h}{h}=\dfrac{2 g R}{v^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{R}{h}=\dfrac{2 g R}{v^{2}}-1=\dfrac{2 g R-v^{2}}{v^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{v^{2} R}{2 g R-v^{2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A mass $m$ on the surface of the Earth is shifted to a target equal to the radius of the Earth. If $R$ is the radius and $M$ is the mass of the Earth, then work done in this process is
explanation:
Gravitational potential energy of the mass $m$ on the surface of the Earth is
${U_1} = - \frac{{GMm}}{R}$
At a height $h( = R)$ from surface of the Earth is
${U_2} = - \frac{{GMm}}{{R + h}} = - \frac{{GMm}}{{2R}}$
$\therefore$ Work done is
$W = {U_2} - {U_1} = - \frac{{GMm}}{{2R}} - \left( { - \frac{{GMm}}{R}} \right)$
$ = - \frac{{GMm}}{{2R}} + \frac{{GMm}}{R} = \frac{{GMm}}{{2R}}$
$ = \frac{{mgR}}{2}\left( {\operatorname{as} \,\,g = \frac{{GM}}{{{R^2}}}} \right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

explanation:

$V=-\dfrac{G M}{a}-\dfrac{G M}{a / 2}=-\dfrac{3 G M}{a}$

question: The kinetic energy of a revolving statellite (mass $m$) at a height equal to thrice the radius of the earth $(R)$ is
explanation:
Kinetic energy of a revolving statellite at a height $h$ above the earth's surface is given as
$K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{G M m}{2(R+h)}$
where, $R$ is radius of the earth, and $m$ is the mass of the satellite.
Given, height of a statellite from the earth surface $h = 3R$,
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore K E=\dfrac{G M m}{2(R+3 R)}=\dfrac{G M m}{8 R}=\dfrac{G M}{R^{2}} \dfrac{m R}{8} \\& \Rightarrow K E=\dfrac{g m R}{8}\left(\because g=\dfrac{G M}{R^{2}}\right)\end{aligned}$

question: The energy required to take a body from the surface of the earth to a height equal to the radius of the earth is '$W$'. The energy required to take this body from the surface of the earth to a height equal to twice the radius of the earth is
explanation:
Work done $=U_{f}-U_{i}$
$\therefore W=-\dfrac{G M m}{2 R}-\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{R}\right)=\dfrac{G M m}{2 R}$
and $W^{\prime}=-\dfrac{G M m}{3 R}-\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{R}\right)=\dfrac{2 G M m}{3 R}$
$\therefore W^{\prime}=\dfrac{4}{3} W$

question: An object of mass '$m$' at a distance of '$20 R$' from the centre of a planet of mass '$M$' and radius '$R$' has an initial velocity '$u$'. The velocity with which the object hits the surface of the planet is ($G$ - Universal gravitational constant)
explanation:
Applying conservation of energy, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952635/preptracker/questions/q_109779_explanation_1764952634630.png $K_{i}+U_{i}=K_{f}+U_{f}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2}+\dfrac{-G M m}{20 R}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\dfrac{-G M m}{R}$
$\therefore v=\left(u^{2}+\dfrac{19 G M}{10 R}\right)^{1 / 2}$

question: Match the following columns related to gravitational potential energy and choose the correct option from the codes given below.
explanation:
$\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{Q}$ : The work done in moving a particle from a height ${h_1}$ to ${h_2}$ is given by the formula $W_{12}=m g\left(h_{2}-h_{1}\right)$, which represents the work done by gravity.
B - P: The gravitational potential energy at a distance r from Earth's surface is given by $W(r)=G \dfrac{M_{E} m}{r}$, where $M_{E}$ is Earth's mass, $m$ is the mass of the particle.
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{R}$ : The potential energy difference between two heights $h_{1}$ and $h_{2}$ is given by${W_{12}} = W\left( {{h_1}} \right) - W\left( {{h_2}} \right)$, which is the change in gravitational potential energy.
D - S: The work done in lifting a particle from a radial distance $r_{1}$ to $r_{2}$ from the center of the Earth is $-G M_{E} m\left(\dfrac{1}{r_{2}}-\dfrac{1}{r_{1}}\right)$, according to the work-energy principle for conservative forces like gravity. Correct option is (1).

options.text: A - Q,        B - P,  C - R,       D - S
A - S,  B - R,  C - P,  D - Q
A - P,  B - S,  C - Q,  D - R
A - R,   B - Q,  C - S,  D - P

subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Match the following columns based on the concepts from Universal Law of Gravitation and choose the correct option from the codes given below:
explanation:
A. Gravitational Potential Energy formula is $U=-\dfrac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r}$ where the potential energy is considered zero at infinity (Q).
B. The formula for Gravitational Force between two masses is $F=-\dfrac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r^{2}}(\mathrm{R})$.
C. A Conservative Force is one where a potential energy function can be associated with the force, such as gravity (S).
D. The Superposition of Forces principle states that the forces can be added up vectorially to find the resultant force (P).
Correct option is (4).

options.text: A - P,    B - Q, C - R,  D - S
A - R,  B - S,  C - P, D - Q
A - S,  B - P,  C - Q, D - R
A - Q,   B - R,  C - S,  D - P

explanation:

B. The Superposition Principle involves the vector addition of individual forces to find the resultant force (S).
C. The Gravitational Force Inside a Sphere on a particle acts toward the center due to the mass interior to the particle's position $(\mathrm{P})$.
D. The Gravitational Potential Energy between two particles is given by $V=-\dfrac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r}$, and it is chosen to be zero at infinity (R).
Correct option is (4).

Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The gravitational potential energy of a system of two masses is directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the distance between them.
The gravitational potential energy at infinity is conventionally taken as zero.
explanation: Statement A is true as the gravitational potential energy $U$ is given by $U=-\dfrac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r}$, where $G$ is the gravitational constant ${m_1}$, and ${m_2}$ are the masses, and $r$ is the distance between their centers. Statement B is also true because it is a common convention to consider the potential energy at infinity as zero to simplify calculations. Correct option is (1).

question: While a person climbs stairs, the gravitational potential energy of the person increases. The source of this energy is
explanation:
Potential energy increases by
$m g\left(h_{2}-h_{1}\right)=m g(h-0)=m g h$
This increase in potential energy is given by
$P E_{f}-P E_{t}=W$
where $W$ is the work done.
Thus, to increase the potential energy the work is done by internal forces inside the body.
So, the correct option is (4).

options.text: work done by the normal force from the steps.
work done by the frictional force from the steps.
work done by the air resistance.
work done by the internal forces within the person's body.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A body of mass $m$ is at a height $R$ from the surface of the earth where $R$ is the radius of the earth. If the body is taken from here to a height of $3 R$ from the surface of the earth, the increase in the gravitational potential energy of the body is ($g$ is acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the earth)
explanation:
At a height $h$ gravitational potential energy is given by
$U = - \frac{{GMm}}{{R + h}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Where $M=$ mass of earth.
Potential energy for $h=R$
${U_1} = - \frac{{GMm}}{{2R}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Potential energy for $h=3 R$
${U_2} = - \frac{{GMm}}{{4R}}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
From Eq. (3) and Eq. (2) increase in potential energy is
$U_{2}-U_{1}=\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{4 R}\right)-\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{2 R}\right)$
$\therefore \Delta U=\dfrac{G M m}{4 R}=\dfrac{m g R}{4}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Four particles each of mass $m$ are placed at four vertices of a rectangle having side length as $3 l_{0}$ and $4 l_{0}$. The magnitude of potential energy of the system in $\dfrac{G m^{2}}{l_{0}}$ is
explanation:
The given situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952614/preptracker/questions/q_109706_explanation_1764952613965.png $A C=B D=\sqrt{\left(4 l_{0}\right)^{2}+\left(3 l_{0}\right)^{2}}=5 l_{0}$
$\therefore$ Potential energy of the system,
$U=U_{A B}+U_{B C}+U_{C D}+U_{D A}+U_{A C}+U_{B D}$
Applying the formula, $U=-G \dfrac{m_{1} m_{2}}{r}$, we get
$U=-\dfrac{G \cdot m \cdot m}{4 l_{0}}-\dfrac{G \cdot m \cdot m}{3 l_{0}}-\dfrac{G \cdot m \cdot m}{4 l_{0}}$
$-\dfrac{G \cdot m \cdot m}{3 l_{0}}-\dfrac{G \cdot m \cdot m}{5 l_{0}}-\dfrac{G \cdot m \cdot m}{5 l_{0}}$
$=-\dfrac{G m^{2}}{l_{0}}\left[\dfrac{1}{4}+\dfrac{1}{3}+\dfrac{1}{4}+\dfrac{1}{3}+\dfrac{1}{5}+\dfrac{1}{5}\right]$
$ = - \frac{{G{m^2}}}{{{l_0}}}\left[ {\frac{1}{2} + \frac{2}{3} + \frac{2}{5}} \right]$
$ = - \frac{{G{m^2}}}{{{l_0}}}\left[ {\frac{{15 + 20 + 12}}{{30}}} \right]$
$=-\dfrac{G m^{2}}{l_{0}}\left(\dfrac{47}{30}\right)$
$|U|=\dfrac{47}{30}\left(\dfrac{G m^{2}}{l_{0}}\right)$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Four identical particles each of mass $m$ are kept at the corners of a square. If the gravitational force exerted on one of the particle by the other particles is $\left(\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2}+1}{32}\right) \dfrac{G m^{2}}{L^{2}}$, then the length of the side of the square is
explanation:
The situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958065/preptracker/questions/q_109703_explanation_1764958064849.png We know that, gravitational force,
$F=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{d^{2}}$
If we calculate net force on 2 , then
$F_{21}=F_{23}=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}}$
and $F_{24}=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{(a \sqrt{2})^{2}}=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{2 a^{2}}$
Now, as forces are vector quantity and angle between $F_{21}$ and $F_{23}$ is $90^{\circ}$.
$\therefore \quad F_{\text {net }}=F_{24}+\sqrt{F_{21}^{2}+F_{23}^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{2 a^{2}}+\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{2 a^{2}}+\sqrt{2\left(\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{2 a^{2}}+\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}} \sqrt{2}$
$=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}+\sqrt{2}\right)=\dfrac{G M^{2}}{a^{2}}\left(\dfrac{1+2 \sqrt{2}}{2}\right)$
According to the question,
$F_{\text {net }}=\left(\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2}+1}{32}\right) \dfrac{G M^{2}}{L^{2}}$
Therefore, $a^{2}=16 L^{2} \Rightarrow a=4 L$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A mass $M$ is split into two parts $m_{0}$ and $M-m_{0}$. These two masses are then separated by a distance $D$. In order to make the gravitational force between the parts maximum, the ratio$\dfrac{m_{0}}{M}$ should be
explanation:
According to given situation, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952624/preptracker/questions/q_109692_explanation_1764952623932.png Again, $m_{0}$ and $M-m_{0}$ are kept at a distance $D$ gravitational force, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952625/preptracker/questions/q_109692_explanation_1764952624729.png $F=\dfrac{G m_{0}\left(M-m_{0}\right)}{D^{2}}$
$F$ will be maximum, when $\dfrac{d F}{d m_{0}}=0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d}{d m_{0}}\left(\dfrac{G m_{0}\left(M-m_{0}\right)}{D^{2}}\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d}{d m_{0}}\left(\dfrac{G m_{0} M-G m_{0}^{2}}{D^{2}}\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{G M-2 G m_{0}}{D^{2}}=0$
$\Rightarrow G M-2 G m_{0}=0 \Rightarrow M-2 m_{0}=0$
$\Rightarrow 2 m_{0}=M \Rightarrow \dfrac{m_{0}}{M}=\dfrac{1}{2}=0.5$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: From the pole of the earth, a body of mass $m$ is imparted a velocity $v_{0}$ directed vertically up. If $M$ is the mass of the earth, $R$ its radius and $g$ is the free-fall acceleration on its surface, then the height $h$ upto which the body will ascent is (neglect air resistance)
explanation:
By using conservation of energy, we get
$\Delta(K E)=\Delta(P E)$
$0-\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}=\dfrac{-G M m}{R}-\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{R+h}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{0}^{2}}{2 G m}=\dfrac{1}{R}-\dfrac{1}{R+h} \Rightarrow \dfrac{R(R+h)}{h}=\dfrac{2 g R^{2}}{v_{0}^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{R}{\left(\dfrac{2 g R}{v_{0}^{2}}-1\right)}$ or $h=\dfrac{R v_{0}^{2}}{2 g R-v_{0}^{2}}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question:
The gravitational field in a region is given by
$\vec g = 5\,N{\rm{/}}kg\,\widehat i + 12\,N{\rm{/}}kg\,\widehat j$. The change in the gravitational potential energy of a particle of mass $1\,kg$ when it is taken from the origin to a point ($7\,m, - 3\,m$) is:
explanation:
$I = - \frac{{dv}}{{dr}}$
$v = - \left[ {\int_0^x {{I_x}} dx + \int_0^y {{I_y}} dy} \right] = - \left[ {{I_x} \cdot x + {I_y} \cdot y} \right]$
$ = - [35 + ( - 36)] = 1\,J{\rm{/}}kg$
$i.e.,$ change in gravitational potential $1\,J{\rm{/}}kg$. Hence change in gravitational potential energy $1\,J$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A body of mass is taken from earth surface to the height $h$ equal to twice the radius of earth $\left(R_{e}\right)$, the increase in potential energy will be ($g=$ acceleration due to gravity on the surface of Earth)
explanation:
The potential energy of a system of two masses is given by, $\quad U=-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{r}$
Potential energy on earth's surface
${U_1} = - \frac{{G{M_e}m}}{R}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Potential energy at height $h=2 R$ is
${U_2} = \frac{{ - G{M_e}m}}{{(2R + R)}} = \frac{{ - G{M_e}m}}{{3R}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Increase in $P . E$
$\Delta U=U_{2}-U_{1}=-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{3 R_{e}}+\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{R_{e}}$
$\Delta U=\dfrac{2}{3} \dfrac{G M_{e} m}{R_{e}}$
As $g=\dfrac{G M_{e}}{R_{e}^{2}}$
$\Delta U=\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{g R_{e}^{2} m}{R_{e}} ; \Delta U=\dfrac{2}{3} m g R_{e}$
So, correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A body is released from a height equal to the radius $(R)$ of the earth. The velocity of the body when it strikes the surface of the earth will be (Given $g=$ acceleration due to gravity on the earth)
explanation:
Applying law of conservation of energy,Total energy of body (K.E. + P.E.) on the earth's surface $=$ Total energy of the body at a height $h$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951569/preptracker/questions/q_35776_explanation_1764951569497.png $T E_{A}=T E_{B}$
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\left(\dfrac{-G M m}{R}\right)=\dfrac{-G M m}{R+h}+\dfrac{1}{2} m(0)^{2}$
or given $\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\dfrac{G M m}{R}=\dfrac{-G M m}{2 R}$
$(\because h=R($ given $)$)
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{G M m}{2 R}$
$\text { or } v=\sqrt{\dfrac{G M}{R}} \text { or } v=\sqrt{\dfrac{G M R}{R^{2}}}$
We know that, $g=\dfrac{G M}{R^{2}}$
$\therefore v=\sqrt{g R}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: The work done in slowly lifting a body from earth's surface to a height $R$ (radius of earth) is equal to two times the work done in lifting the same body from earth's surface to a height $h$. Here $h$ is equal to
explanation:
Work done = increase in gravitational potential energy
$W_{R \rightarrow 2 R}=2\left(W_{R \rightarrow R+h}\right)$
$\left(U_{f}-U_{i}\right)=2\left(U_{f}-U_{i}\right)$
$-\dfrac{G M m}{R+R}+\dfrac{G M m}{R}=2\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{R+h}+\dfrac{G M m}{R}\right)$
$\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{R}{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: The gravitational field in a region is given by $(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}) N / k g$. What is the work done by an external agent to slowly shift a particle of mass $10\,kg$ from the point $(0,0)$ to a point $(5\,m,4\,m)$?
explanation:
The work done by external agent is
$W=m \Delta V=m(\vec{E} . \Delta \vec{r})$
The displacement is
$\Delta \vec r = 5\hat i + 4\hat j$
$W = 10(2\hat i + 2\hat j) \cdot (5\hat i + 4\hat j)$
$ = 10(10 + 8) = 180\,J.$
subject: Physics

question: What is the maximum height attained by a body projected with a velocity equal to one-third of the escape velocity from the surface of the earth? (Radius of earth $=R$ )
explanation:
From conservation of energy,
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}-\dfrac{G M m}{R}=\dfrac{-G M m}{R+h} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m \dfrac{1}{9} \dfrac{2 G M}{R}=\dfrac{G M m h}{R+H}\end{aligned}$
$9 h=R+h \Rightarrow h=R / 8$

question: An asteroid of mass $m$ is approaching earth, initially at a distance of $10 R_{e}$, with speed $v_{i}$. It hits the earth with a speed $v_{f}\left(R_{e}\right.$ and $M_{e}$ are radius and mass of earth), then
explanation:
Applying law of conservation of energy for asteroid at a distance $10 R_{e}$ and at earth's surface,
${\left( {{E_K}} \right)_i} + {U_i} = {\left( {{E_K}} \right)_f} + {U_f}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951507/preptracker/questions/q_35381_explanation_1764951506756.png Now, $\left(E_{K}\right)_{i}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{i}^{2}$ and $U_{i}=-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{10 R_{e}}$
$\left(E_{K}\right)_{f}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{f}^{2}$ and $U_{f}=-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{R_{e}}$
On substituting these values in Eq. (1), we get $\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{i}^{2}-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{10 R_{e}}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{f}^{2}-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{R_{e}}$
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{f}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{i}^{2}+\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{R_{e}}-\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{10 R_{e}}$
$\Rightarrow v_{f}^{2}=v_{i}^{2}+\dfrac{2 G M_{e}}{R_{e}}-\dfrac{2 G M_{e}}{10 R_{e}}$$\therefore v_{f}^{2}=v_{i}^{2}+\dfrac{2 G M_{e}}{R_{e}}\left(1-\dfrac{1}{10}\right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: The gravitational field in a region is given by $I = (4\hat i + \hat j)Nk{g^{ - 1}}$. Work done by this field is zero when a particle is moved along the line
explanation:
Work done by the gravitational field is zero, when displacement is perpendicular to gravitational field. Here, gravitational field, $I=4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}$. If $\theta_{1}$ is the angle which field makes with positive $x$-axis, then
$\tan \theta_{1}=\dfrac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \theta_{1}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)=14^{\circ} 6^{\prime} \text { (Clockwise). }$
If $\theta_{2}$ is the angle which the line $y+4 x=6$ makes with positive $x$-axis, then $\theta_{2}=\tan ^{-1}(-4)=-75^{\circ} 56^{\prime}$ (Anti clockwise)
so $\left|\theta_{1}\right|+\left|\theta_{2}\right|=90^{\circ}$
i.e., the line $y+4 x=6$ is perpendicular to $\boldsymbol{I}$.
subject: Physics

explanation:

$\begin{aligned}& W_{2}=U_{\infty}-U_{\text {Surface }} \\& \dfrac{W_{1}}{W_{2}}=\dfrac{-\dfrac{G M}{R}-\left(-\dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{G M}{R}\right)}{0-\left(-\dfrac{G M}{R}\right)}=\dfrac{1}{2}\end{aligned}$

question:
A particle is projected from point $A$, that is at a distance 4$R$ from the centre of the Earth, with speed $v_{1}$ in a direction making $30^{\circ}$ with the line joining the centre of the Earth and point $A$, as shown. Find the speed $v_{1}$ of particle (in $m/s$) if particle passes grazing the surface of the earth. Consider gravitational interaction only between earth and the particle.
(use $\frac{{GM}}{R} = 6.4 \times {10^7}\,{m^2}/{s^2}$)
explanation:
Conserving angular momentum of the particle w.r.to the centre of the earth.
$m\left(v_{1} \cos 60\right) 4 R=m v_{2} R \Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{2}}{v_{1}}=2 .$
Conserving energy of the system:
$\begin{aligned}& -\dfrac{G M m}{4 R}+\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}=-\dfrac{G M m}{R}+\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{2}^{2} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} v_{2}^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} v_{1}^{2}=\dfrac{3}{4} \dfrac{G M}{R} \Rightarrow v_{1}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{G M}{R} \\& \Rightarrow v_{1}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{64 \times 10^{6}}=\dfrac{8000}{\sqrt{2}} m / s\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: The potential energy of gravitational interaction of a point mass $m$ and a thin uniform rod of mass $M$ and length $l$, if they are located along a straight line at distance $a$ (From the nearest end of the rod) from each other is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955850/preptracker/questions/q_35173_explanation_1764955850376.png Mass of small element of length, $d M=\dfrac{M}{l} d x$
The gravitational potential energy between this element and point mass is
$dU = \frac{{ - GmdM}}{x} = - \frac{{Gm\left( {\frac{M}{l}dx} \right)}}{x}$
$\therefore U = - \frac{{GMm}}{l}\int\limits_a^{a + \ell } {\frac{{dx}}{x}} $
$U = - \frac{{GMm}}{l}1n\left( {\frac{{a + \ell }}{a}} \right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A body of mass $m$ $kg$ starts falling from a point 2$R$ above the Earth's surface. Its kinetic energy when it has fallen to a point ' $R$ ' above the Earth's surface [$R$-Radius of Earth, $M$-Mass of Earth, $G$-Gravitational Constant]
explanation:
Gravitational Potential energy
$\begin{aligned}& U=\dfrac{-G M m}{r}=\dfrac{-G M m}{R+h} \\& U_{\text {initial }}=-\dfrac{G M m}{3 R} \text { and } U_{\text {final }}=\dfrac{-G M m}{2 R}\end{aligned}$
Loss in $P E=$ gain in $KE$ $=\dfrac{G M m}{2 R}-\dfrac{G M m}{3 R}=\dfrac{G M m}{6 R}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Three identical masses $m$ each are kept at the corners of an equilateral triangle of side $a$. The work done by external agent to increase the side of the triangle from $a$ to $2 a$, is
explanation:
Work done by the external agent is
$\begin{aligned}& W=U_{f}-U_{i} \\& U_{i}=3\left[\dfrac{-G m^{2}}{a}\right] \\& U_{f}=3\left[\dfrac{-G m^{2}}{2 a}\right] \\& W=-3 \dfrac{G m^{2}}{2 a}+\dfrac{3 G m^{2}}{a}=\dfrac{3 G m^{2}}{2 a}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A rocket of mass $M$ is launched vertically from the surface of the earth with an initial speed $V$. Assuming the radius of the earth to be $R$ and negligible air resistance, the maximum height attained by the rocket above the surface of the earth is
explanation:
From conservation of
$ energy\,\frac{1}{2}m{V^2} = G{M_e}m\left( {\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{{R + h}}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\operatorname{Also} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} g = \frac{{G{M_e}}}{{{R^2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (2)$
On solving (1) and (2)
$h=\dfrac{V^{2} R^{2}}{2 G M-V^{2} R}=\dfrac{R}{\left(\dfrac{2 g R}{V^{2}}-1\right)}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

explanation: $\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{e}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m 2 g R=m g R$.

question: The work done by an external agent to shift a point mass from infinity to the centre of the earth is ' $W$ '. Then choose the correct relation.
explanation:
The work done by the external agent is
$\begin{aligned}& W=U_{f}-U_{i} \\& U_{i}=\dfrac{-G M m}{\infty}=0 \\& U_{f}=\dfrac{-3 G M m}{2 R} \\& W=(-) v e\end{aligned}$
So option (3) is correct.

question: A particle falls from infinity to the earth. Its velocity on reaching the earth of radius $R$ is
explanation:
$T \cdot E_{i}=T \cdot E_{f}$
$\begin{aligned}& -\dfrac{G M m}{\infty}+\dfrac{1}{2} m(0)^{2}=\dfrac{-G M m}{R}+\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \\& v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M}{R}}=\sqrt{2 g R}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Two identical thin rings each of radius $R$ are coaxially placed at a distance $R$. If the rings have a uniform mass distribution and each has mass $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ respectively, then the work done in moving a mass $m$ from centre of one ring to that of the other is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955787/preptracker/questions/q_34749_explanation_1764955786740.png $V_{A}=\left(\right.$ Potential at $A$ due to $\left.m_{1}\right)+($ Potential at $A$ due to $m_{2}$ )
$\Rightarrow V_{A}=-\dfrac{G m_{1}}{R}-\dfrac{G m_{2}}{\sqrt{2} R}$
Similarly,
$V_{B}=\left(\right.$ Potential at $B$ due to $\left.m_{1}\right)+($ Potential at $B$ due to $m_{2}$ )
$\Rightarrow V_{B}=-\dfrac{G m_{2}}{R}-\dfrac{G m_{1}}{\sqrt{2} R}$
Since, $W_{A \rightarrow B}=m\left(V_{B}-V_{A}\right)$
$=\dfrac{G m\left(m_{1}-m_{2}\right)(\sqrt{2}-1)}{\sqrt{2} R}$
$U=-\dfrac{G M M}{R}=-\dfrac{G M^{2}}{R}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: The self gravitational potential energy of a spherical shell of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is
explanation:
Assume that the total mass $M$ is present at infinity. We bring the total mass and form like a sphere. Let $m$ is the mass of the shell having radius $R$. To bring further mass $dm$ from infinity the work done by the external agent is
$d{W_{{\text{ext }}}} = dU = {U_f} - {U_i} = {U_R} - {U_\infty }$
$d{W_{{\text{ext }}}} = \left( {\frac{{ - Gm}}{R}{\text{d m}} - \frac{{Gmdm}}{\infty }} \right)$
${W_{{\text{ext }}}} = \frac{{ - G}}{R}\int_0^M m dm = \frac{{ - G{M^2}}}{{2R}}$
${W_{{\text{ext }}}} = {U_{{\text{Self }}}} = \frac{{ - G{M^2}}}{{2R}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Self Energy of Spherical Bodies

question: The potential energy of interaction between the semi-circular ring of mass $M$ and radius $R$, and the particle of mass $M$ placed at the centre of curvature of the semi-circular arc is
explanation:
The potential energy of the system
$U = - \frac{{GMM}}{R} = - \frac{{G{M^2}}}{R}$

question: Distance between the centres of two stars is $10 a$. The masses of these stars are $M$ and 16$M$ and their radii $a$ and $2 a$ respectively. A body of mass $m$ is fired straight from the surface of the largger star towards the smaller star. The minimum initial speed for the body to reach the surface of smaller star is
explanation:
First we find a point where the resultant field due to both is zero. Let the point $P$ be at a distance $x$ from centre of bigger star. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956918/preptracker/questions/q_34738_explanation_1764956918043.png $\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{G(16 M)}{x^{2}}=\dfrac{G M}{(10 a-x)^{2}} \\& \Rightarrow \quad x=8 a \quad\left(\text { from } O_{1}\right)\end{aligned}$
i.e., once the body reaches $P$, the gravitational pull of attraction due to $M$ takes the lead to make $m$ move towards it automatically. A minimum $KE$ has to be imparted to$m$ from surface of 16$M$ such that it is just able to reach point $P$. By law of conservation of energy.
(Total mechanical energy at $A$)
$=($ Total mechanical energy at $P$)
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v_{\min }^{2}+\left[\dfrac{-G(16 M) m}{2 a}-\dfrac{G(M) m}{8 a}\right] \\& =0+\left[\dfrac{-G M m}{2 a}-\dfrac{G(16 M) m}{8 a}\right] \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v_{\min }^{2}=\dfrac{G M m}{8 a}(45)\end{aligned}$

$\Rightarrow \quad v_{\min }=\dfrac{3}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{5 G M}{a}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: The gravitational field in a region is given by $\vec g = 5\,N/kg\hat i + 12\,N/kg\hat j$. The change in the gravitational potential energy of a particle of mass 1 $kg$ when it is taken from the origin to a point $(7 m,-3 m)$ is:
explanation:
Gravitational field, $\overrightarrow g = \left( {5\hat i + 12\hat j} \right)N/kg$
$g = - \frac{{dV}}{{dr}}$
$V = - \left[ {\int\limits_0^x {{g_x}dx + \int\limits_0^y {{g_x}dy} } } \right] = - \left[ {{g_x}.x + {g_x}.y} \right]$
i.e., change in gravitational potential 1$J$/$kg$.
Hence change in gravitational potential energy 1 $J$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Given that mass of the earth is $M$ and its radius is $R$. A body is dropped from a height equal to the radius of the earth. When it reaches the ground its velocity will be :-
explanation:
From conservation of energy
$\begin{aligned}& T \cdot E_{i}=T \cdot E_{f} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{-G M m}{2 R}+0=\dfrac{-G M m}{R}+\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \\& \Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\dfrac{G M}{R}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Four particles each of mass $m$ are placed at the vertices of a square of side $l$. The potential energy of the system is
explanation:
The four particles are shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951292/preptracker/questions/q_34624_explanation_1764951291701.png From figure
$\begin{aligned}& A B=B C=C D=A D=l \\& \therefore A C=B D=\sqrt{l^{2}+l^{2}}=l \sqrt{2}\end{aligned}$
Total potential energy of the system of four particles each of mass $m$ placed at the vertices $A, B, C$ and $D$ of a square is
$\begin{aligned}& U=\left(\dfrac{G \times m \times m}{A B}\right)+\left(-\dfrac{G \times m \times m}{A C}\right) \\& +\left(-\dfrac{G \times m \times m}{A D}\right)+\left(-\dfrac{G \times m \times m}{B C}\right) \\& +\left(-\dfrac{G \times m \times m}{B D}\right)+\left(-\dfrac{G \times m \times m}{C D}\right) \\& =-\dfrac{4 G m^{2}}{l}-\dfrac{2 G m^{2}}{l \sqrt{2}}=-\dfrac{2 G m^{2}}{l}\left(2+\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Two bodies of masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ are initially at rest at infinite distance apart. They are then allowed to move towards each other under mutual gravitational attraction. Their relative velocity of approach at a separation of $r$ between them is
explanation:
Apply the principle of conservation of linear momentum and conservation of energy.
Let velocities of these bodies at $r$ distance from each other be $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ respectively.
By conservation of momentum
${m_1}{v_1} - {m_2}{v_2} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow {m_1}{v_1} = {m_2}{v_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
From conservation of energy
$K{E_i} + {U_i} = K{E_f} + {U_f}$
$0 = \frac{{ - G{m_1}{m_2}}}{r} + KE$
$\frac{{G{m_1}{m_2}}}{r} = \frac{1}{2}{m_1}v_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}{m_2}v_2^2\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) & (2)
$\begin{gathered}v_{1}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G m_{2}^{2}}{r\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)}}, v_{2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G m_{1}^{2}}{r\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)}} \\\therefore v_{\text {rel }}=\left|v_{1}\right|+\left|v_{2}\right|=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G}{r}\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A shell of mass \(M\) and radius \(R\) has a point mass \(m\) placed at a distance \(r\) from its centre. The gravitational potential energy \(U(r)\) vs \(r\) will be
explanation:
$U=m V$
$\begin{array}{ll}U=-\dfrac{G M m}{R} & (r \leq R) \\U=-\dfrac{G M m}{r} & (r \geq R)\end{array}$

question: A satellite of mass $m$ is orbiting the earth (of radius $R$ ) at a height $h$ from its surface. The total energy of the satellite in terms of $g_{o}$, the value of acceleration due to gravity at the earth's surface is
explanation:
Total energy $=-\dfrac{G M m}{2(R+h)}$
$=-\dfrac{G M}{2 R^{2}} \dfrac{R^{2} m}{(R+h)}=-\dfrac{g_{o} m R^{2}}{2(R+h)}$

question: A particle of mass ' $m$ ' is projected with a velocity $u = k{v_e}(k < 1)$ from the surface of the earth. $\left( {{v_e}} \right. = $ escape velocity) The maximum height above the surface reached by the particle is
explanation:
Let $h$ be the maximum height reached by the particle.
From conservation of energy
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{-G M m}{h}+\dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2}=-\dfrac{G M m}{R+h} \\& -\dfrac{G M m^{2}}{h}=\dfrac{G M m}{h}+\dfrac{1}{2} m k^{2} \dfrac{2 G M}{R} \\& \dfrac{-1}{R}+\dfrac{k^{2}}{R}=\dfrac{-1}{R+h} \\& h=\dfrac{R k^{2}}{1-k^{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question:
Match Column-I with Column-II
A. Gravitational constant
B. Gravitational potential energy
C. Gravitational potential
D. Gravitational intensity
P. $[{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}]$
Q. $[{M^{ - 1}}{L^3}{T^{ - 2}}]$
R. $[L{T^{ - 2}}]$
S. $[M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}]$
explanation:
Gravitational constant $ = \left[ {{M^{ - 1}}\,{L^3}\,{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$ Gravitational potential energy $ = \left[ {M{L^2}\,{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$ Gravitational potential $ = \left[ {{L^2}\,{T^{ - 2}}} \right]$
Gravitational intensity $ = \left[ {L{T^{ - 2}}} \right] = $ (same as of $g$)
Since weight $W$ is also force
$W = mg \Rightarrow $
$g = \frac{W}{m}$
gravitational intensity ${I_g} = (F/m)$

options.text: A - Q,	B - S,	     C - P,	D - R
A - Q,	B - S,	     C - R,	D - P
A - S,	B - Q,	     C - P,	D - R

subject: Physics

question: A body is thrown from the surface of the earth with velocity $u\,m/s$. The maximum height in metre above the surface of the earth up to which it will reach is ( where $R=$ radius of earth, $g=$ acceleration due to gravity)
explanation:
Given, velocity of the body $ = u\,m/s$
Using conservation of energy between surface and maximum height,
${U_1} + {K_1} = {U_2} + {K_2}$
$-\dfrac{G M m}{R}+\dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2}=\left(-\dfrac{G M m}{R+h}\right)+0$
$\dfrac{G M}{R+h}=\dfrac{G M}{R}-\dfrac{u^{2}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{R+h}{G M}=\dfrac{2 R}{2 G M-R u^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{2 G M R}{2 G M-R u^{2}}-R$
$ \Rightarrow h = \frac{{{R^2}{u^2}}}{{2GM - R{u^2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
But $g=\dfrac{G M}{R^{2}}$
$ \Rightarrow GM = g{R^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Substituting the value of eq.(2) in eq.(1), we get
$h=\dfrac{R u^{2}}{2 g R-u^{2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A body of mass $m$ is kept at a small height $h$ above the ground. If the radius of the earth is $R$ and its mass is $M$, the potential energy of the body and earth system (with $h=\infty$ being the reference position) is
explanation:
On the surface of earth $U=-\dfrac{G M m}{R}$
At height $h( < < R)$, increase in potential energy is $mgh$
$\therefore {U_h} = - \frac{{GMm}}{R} + mgh$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A body is thrown from the surface of the earth with velocity $v\,m/s$. The maximum height above the earth's surface up to which it will reach is ($R=$ radius of earth, $g=$ acceleration due to gravity)
explanation:
Let the maximum height where the velocity of body becomes zero be $h$.
Applying principle of conservation of energy, we get
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\dfrac{G M m}{R}=\dfrac{1}{2} m(0)^{2}-\dfrac{G M m}{(R+h)}$
[ where $m=$ mass of body and $M=$ mass of earth]
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=G M m\left(\dfrac{1}{R}-\dfrac{1}{R+h}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{h}{R(R+h)}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{2 G M}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{h}{R(R+h)}=\dfrac{v^{2} R}{2 g R^{2}}\left(\because G M=g R^{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{R+h}{h}=\dfrac{2 g R}{v^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{R}{h}=\dfrac{2 g R}{v^{2}}-1=\dfrac{2 g R-v^{2}}{v^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{v^{2} R}{2 g R-v^{2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: A mass $m$ on the surface of the Earth is shifted to a target equal to the radius of the Earth. If $R$ is the radius and $M$ is the mass of the Earth, then work done in this process is
explanation:
Gravitational potential energy of the mass $m$ on the surface of the Earth is
${U_1} = - \frac{{GMm}}{R}$
At a height $h( = R)$ from surface of the Earth is
${U_2} = - \frac{{GMm}}{{R + h}} = - \frac{{GMm}}{{2R}}$
$\therefore$ Work done is
$W = {U_2} - {U_1} = - \frac{{GMm}}{{2R}} - \left( { - \frac{{GMm}}{R}} \right)$
$ = - \frac{{GMm}}{{2R}} + \frac{{GMm}}{R} = \frac{{GMm}}{{2R}}$
$ = \frac{{mgR}}{2}\left( {\operatorname{as} \,\,g = \frac{{GM}}{{{R^2}}}} \right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: The kinetic energy of a revolving statellite (mass $m$) at a height equal to thrice the radius of the earth $(R)$ is
explanation:
Kinetic energy of a revolving statellite at a height $h$ above the earth's surface is given as
$K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\dfrac{G M m}{2(R+h)}$
where, $R$ is radius of the earth, and $m$ is the mass of the satellite.
Given, height of a statellite from the earth surface $h = 3R$,
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore K E=\dfrac{G M m}{2(R+3 R)}=\dfrac{G M m}{8 R}=\dfrac{G M}{R^{2}} \dfrac{m R}{8} \\& \Rightarrow K E=\dfrac{g m R}{8}\left(\because g=\dfrac{G M}{R^{2}}\right)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Gravitational Potential Energy of a Group of Particles/Bodies

question: Match the terms related to planetary motion and their description. Choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:

B. The farthest point from the Sun in the orbit is the aphelion $(\mathrm{R})$.
C. The major axis of an ellipse is the line segment that includes both the perihelion and aphelion, running through the center and both foci of the ellipse (S).
D. The area swept by the line segment joining a planet and the Sun per unit time is a constant, which can be referred to as the areal velocity (P). Correct option is (4).

question: Match the following Kepler's Laws with their corresponding statements and choose the correct option:
explanation:
A. The Law of Orbits states that planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun at one focus (Q).
B. The Law of Areas indicates that a line segment joining a planet and the Sun sweeps out equal areas during equal intervals of time (R).
C. The Law of Periods shows that the square of a planet's orbital period is directly proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of its orbit (S).
D. The Shape of Planetary Orbits is elliptical, which was a significant deviation from the circular orbits proposed before Kepler (P).
Correct option is (4).

question: Two stars each of mass $M$, are orbiting in a circle of radius $R$, their orbital time period is proportional to
explanation:
Given, mass of each star is $M$, orbital radius is $R$ and time period is $T$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952611/preptracker/questions/q_109702_explanation_1764952610896.png Under the action of their mutual gravitational force of attraction, the stars are moving in circular path.
By using Kepler's second law of planetary motion,
$\begin{gathered}T^{2} \propto R^{3} \\\therefore \quad T \propto R^{3 / 2}\end{gathered}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A planet is revolving around the sun. Which of the following is correct statement?
explanation:
Speed of planet is more, when it is near to sun. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958059/preptracker/questions/q_109698_explanation_1764958059014.png So, in arc $D A B$, it is moving at high speed than when it is in arc $B C D$.
$\therefore$ Time taken in travelling arc $D A B$ is less than that for $B C D$.
So, the correct option is (1).

options.text: The time taken in travelling $D A B$ is less than that for $B C D$.
The time taken in travelling $D A B$ is greater than that for $B C D$.
The time taken in travelling $C D A$ is less than that for $A B C$.
The time taken in travelling $C D A$ is greater than that for $A B C$.

subject: Physics

question:
A planet is moving in an elliptical orbit around the sun. The work done on the planet by the gravitational force of the sun
(I) is zero in no part of the motion.
(II) is zero in some parts of the orbit.
(III) is zero in one complete revolution.
(IV) is zero in any small part of the orbit.
Which of the following is true?
explanation:
Gravitational force is a conservative force, hence work done in a complete cycle will be zero.
Again, since, work done, $W=\vec{F} \cdot d \vec{r}=m \vec{a} \cdot d \vec{r}$
If a $\perp d r$, then $W=0$
Clearly, the moments when displacement vector makes $90^{\circ}$ angle with acceleration, work done will be zero.
Work done on the planet is zero at perigee and apogee
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: The distance between the Sun and the Earth is $1.6 \times 10^{11} {~m}$ and the radius of the Earth is $6.4 \times 10^{6} {~m}$. The ratio of the angular momentum of Earth around the Sun to the angular momentum around its own axis is approximately (Assume Earth as a solid sphere with uniform mass density and rotates around the Sun in a circular path.)
explanation:
Angular momentum of Earth around the
$\operatorname{Sun}, L_{1}=M_{e} v_{1} r_{1}=M_{e} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi r_{1}}{T_{1}} \cdot r_{1}$
$\therefore L_{1}=M_{e} \times \dfrac{2 \pi\left(1.6 \times 10^{11}\right)^{2}}{365 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60}$
$\Rightarrow L_{1}=5.098 \times M_{e} \times 10^{15}$
Angular momentum of Earth about its own axis,
$L_{2}=I \omega$
Here, $I=\dfrac{2}{5} M_{e} R_{e}^{2}$.
or $\quad I=\dfrac{2}{5} \times M_{e} \times\left(6.4 \times 10^{6}\right)^{2}$
$\simeq 16.384 \times M_{e} \times 10^{12}$
The earth's angular velocity,
$\omega=\dfrac{2 \pi}{1 \text { day }}$
$=\dfrac{2 \pi}{24 \times 60 \times 10} \simeq 7.3 \times 10^{-5} {~s}^{-1}$
$\therefore \quad L_{2}=16.384 \times M_{e} \times 10^{10} \times 7.3 \times 10^{-5}$
$=1.196 \times M_{e} \times 10^{9}$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{L_{1}}{L_{2}}=\dfrac{5.098 \times 10^{15}}{1.196 \times 10^{9}}=4.3 \times 10^{6}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: The radius of Martian orbit around the Sun is about 4 times the radius of the orbit of Mercury. The Martian year is 687 Earth days. Then which of the following is the length of 1 year on Mercury?
explanation:
Applying Kepler's ${3^{{\text{rd }}}}$ law: $T^{2} \propto R^{3}$
Radius of Martian orbit, $R^{\prime}=4 R$
$\left(\dfrac{T^{\prime}}{T}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{R^{\prime}}{R}\right)^{3}=\left(\dfrac{4 R}{R}\right)^{3}=64 \Rightarrow \dfrac{T^{\prime}}{T}=8$
$\therefore$ Length of 1 year on Mercury $=T=\dfrac{T^{\prime}}{8}=\dfrac{687}{8}$
$=85.88$ days $\, \approx \,88$ days.
subject: Physics

question: If $r_{p}, v_{p}, L_{p}$ and $r_{a}, v_{a}, L_{a}$ are radii, velocities and angular momenta of a planet at perihelion and aphelion of its elliptical orbit around the Sun respectively, then
explanation:
Orbit of the planet is described in following diagram. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952619/preptracker/questions/q_109678_explanation_1764952619123.png Angular momentum of a planet about the centre of sun is conserved.
So, ${L_p} = {L_A} \Rightarrow \,m{v_p}{r_p} = m{v_A}{r_A} \Rightarrow {v_p} > {v_A}$
So correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A satellite is nine times closer to earth compared to moon. If time period of moon is 27 days, then period of satellite is
explanation:
Using Kepler's ${3^{rd{\text{ }}}}$ law, time period of revolution varies with radius of orbit as :
$T^{2} \alpha r^{3}$
$\therefore {\left( {\frac{{{T_2}}}{{{T_1}}}} \right)^2} = {\left( {\frac{{{r_2}}}{{{r_1}}}} \right)^3}$
${T_1} = {\rm{Time}}\,\,{\rm{period}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{moon}}$
${T_2} = {\rm{Time}}\,\,{\rm{period}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{satellite}}$
$\Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{r / 9}{r}\right)^{3}$
$\Rightarrow \quad T_{2}=T_{1}\left(\dfrac{1}{3^{2}}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}} \Rightarrow T_{2}=27 \times \dfrac{1}{27}=1$ day
So, correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A planet takes 200 days to complete one revolution around the Sun. If the distance of  the planet from Sun is reduced to one fourth of the original distance, how many days will it take to complete one revolution?
explanation:
Given: $T_{1}=200$ days $r_{1}=r, r_{2}=\dfrac{r}{4}$
By Kepler's third law $T^{2} \propto r^{3}$
$\begin{aligned}& \left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{3} ;\left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{200}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{r}{4 r}\right)^{3} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{T_{2}}{200}=\left(\dfrac{1}{64}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \\& T_{2}=\dfrac{200}{8}=25 \text { days }\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Two planets $A$ and $B$ having masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ move around the sun in circular orbits of $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ radii, respectively. If angular momentum of $A$ is $L$ and that of $B$ is $3 L$, the ratio of time $\operatorname{period}\left(\dfrac{T_{A}}{T_{B}}\right)$ is
explanation:
Given, $L_{B}=3 L_{A}$
$3 m_{1} v_{1} r_{1}=m_{2} v_{2} r_{2}$ $\quad \quad (1)$
For satellite, $\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}=\dfrac{G M m}{r^{2}}$
$v=\sqrt{\dfrac{G M}{r}}, v \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{r}}$ $\quad \quad (2)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}}$
By (1) and (2) $\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{m_{2}}{3 m_{1}}\right)^{2}$
$\left(\dfrac{T_{A}}{T_{B}}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)^{3}$ (Using Kepler's law of periods)
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{T_{A}}{T_{B}}=\dfrac{1}{27}\left(\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}\right)^{3} \quad\left(\because \dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{m_{2}}{3 m_{1}}\right)^{2}\right)$
subject: Physics

question: A light planet is revolving around a massive star in a circular orbit of radius $R$ with a period of revolution $T$. If the force of attraction between planet and star is proportional to $R^{-3 / 2}$ then choose the correct option
explanation:
Force of attraction between planet and star, to revolve the planet in a circular orbit is
$\therefore F=\dfrac{G M m}{R^{2}}=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R}$
As per question $F \propto R^{-3 / 2}$
So, $\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R} \propto R^{-3 / 2} \Rightarrow m R \omega^{2} \propto R^{-3 / 2}$
$\therefore \omega^{2} \propto R^{-3 / 2} \cdot R^{-1} \Rightarrow \omega^{2} \propto R^{-5 / 2}$
$\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right)^{2} \propto \dfrac{1}{R^{5 / 2}}\left(\because \omega=\dfrac{2 \pi}{T}\right)$
So, $T^{2} \propto R^{5 / 2}$
subject: Physics

question: The earth moves around the sun in an elliptical orbit (as shown in figure). The ratio $\frac{{OA}}{{OB}} = x.$ The ratio of the speed of the earth at $B$ to that at $A$ is found to be $x^{N}$. The value of $N$ is
explanation:
Applying conservation of angular momentum at position $A$ and $B,$
$m{v_A} \times OA = m{v_B} \times OB$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{v_B}}}{{{v_A}}} = \frac{{OA}}{{OB}} = x$
$ \Rightarrow {x^N} = x$
$ \Rightarrow N = 1.00$
subject: Physics

question: A particle of mass $m$ is subjected to an attractive central force of magnitude $\frac{k}{{{r^2}}},k$ being $\mathrm{a}$ constant. If at instant when the particle is at an extreme position in its closed orbit, at a distance ' $a$ ' from the centre of force, its speed is $\sqrt {(k{\rm{/}}2ma)} ,$ if the distance of other extreme position is $b$. The ratio of $a / b$ is
explanation:
$F = - \frac{k}{{{r^2}}};U = - \int {Fdr} = \int {\frac{k}{{{r^2}}}} dr = - \frac{k}{r}$
From conservation of momentum (angular)$m{v_1}a = m{v_2}\,b$
$ \Rightarrow {v_2} = \frac{a}{b}{v_1} = \frac{a}{b}\sqrt {\frac{k}{{2ma}}} $
Now, from law of conservation of energy,
${K_1} + {U_1} = {K_2} + {U_2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}mv_1^2 - \frac{k}{a} = \frac{1}{2}mv_2^2 - \frac{k}{b}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}m\left( {\frac{k}{{2ma}}} \right) - \left( {\frac{k}{a}} \right)$
$ = \frac{1}{2}m{\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right)^2}\left( {\frac{k}{{2ma}}} \right) - \frac{k}{b}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{ - 3k}}{{4a}} = \frac{{ak}}{{4\,{b^2}}} - \frac{k}{b}$
$ \Rightarrow {b^2} - \frac{{4a}}{3}\,b + \frac{{{a^2}}}{3} = 0$
Solving $b = \frac{{\frac{{4a}}{3} - \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{{4a}}{3}} \right)}^2} - 4\left( {\frac{{{a^2}}}{3}} \right)} }}{2}$
$\therefore \quad b = \frac{{\frac{{4a}}{3} - \frac{{2a}}{3}}}{2} = \frac{a}{3}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{a}{b} = 3$
subject: Physics

question: If the earth is at one - fourth of its present distance from the sun, the duration of the year will be
explanation:
According to Kepler's third law,
${\Rightarrow T^{2} \propto r^{3}}$
Given ${: \dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{4}}$
$\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{3 / 2}=\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)^{3 / 2}=\dfrac{1}{8}$${\Rightarrow T_{2}=\dfrac{1}{8} T_{1}=\dfrac{1}{8}^{\text {th }}}$ of the present year.
So correct option is (4)
subject: Physics

question: A planet revolves in elliptical orbit around the sun. The linear speed of the planet will be maximum at
explanation: Here, angular momentum is conserved, i.e. $L=I \omega=$ constant. At point $A$, the moment of inertia $I$ is least, since $I \propto \dfrac{l}{\omega}$, so angular speed $\omega$ and the linear speed of the planet at is maximum.
subject: Physics

question: The earth revolves around the sun in one year. If the distance between them becomes double, then the new time period of revolution will be
explanation:
Given, $T_{1}=1 y r$,
$R_{1}=R, R_{2}=2 R$
According to Kepler's third law of planetary motion, $T^{2} \propto R^{3}$
where, $R$ is the distance between earth and sun.
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore=\left(\dfrac{R}{2 R}\right)^{3}=\dfrac{1}{8} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \\& \Rightarrow T_{2}=2 \sqrt{2} T_{1}=2 \sqrt{2} y r\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question:
Consider a satellite orbiting the earth as shown in the figure below. Let $L_{a}$ and $L_{p}$ represent the angular momentum of the satellite about the earth when at aphelion and perihelion, respectively. Consider the following relations,
I. $L_{a}=L_{p}$
II. $\mathrm{L}_{a}=-L_{p}$
III. $r_{a} \times L_{a}=r_{p} \times L_{p}$
Which of the above relations is/are true?
explanation:
Using the law of conservation of angular momentum,
Angular momentum of planet at
$p=$ Angular momentum of planet at $a$
$\begin{array}{lc}\Rightarrow & L_{p}=L_{a} \\\Rightarrow & r_{a}>r_{p} \\\Rightarrow & r_{a} \times L_{a}>r_{p} \times L_{p}\end{array}$
subject: Physics

question: A planet of radius $R$ has a time period of revolution $T$ Find time period of a planet of radius $9 R$ ?
explanation:
By Kepler's third law of planetory motion,
$\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}$
Given, $T_{1}=T, a_{1}=R, T_{2}=$ ?, $a_{2}=9 R$
$\begin{aligned}\therefore \quad \dfrac{T}{T_{2}} & =\left(\dfrac{R}{9 R}\right)^{3 / 2}, \dfrac{T}{T_{2}}=\left[\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^{2}\right]^{3 / 2} \\\dfrac{T}{T_{2}} & =\dfrac{1}{27} \Rightarrow T_{2}=27 T\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The orbital angular momentum of a satellite revolving at a distance $r$ from the centre is $L$. If the distance is increased to $16 r$, then the new angular momentum will be
explanation:
${L=m v r=m \times \sqrt{\dfrac{G M}{r}} \times r}$
${=m \sqrt{G M r} \therefore L \propto \sqrt{r}}$
Now, ${\dfrac{L^{\prime}}{L}=\sqrt{\dfrac{r^{\prime}}{r}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{16 r}{r}} \Rightarrow L^{\prime}=4 L}$
subject: Physics

question: The time period of a satellite of earth is 24 hours. If the separation between the earth and the satellite is decreased to one fourth of the previous value, then its new time period will become
explanation:
Given: $T_{1}=24 \mathrm{hrs}, r_{1}=r, r_{2}=\dfrac{r}{4}, T_{2}=$?
By Kepler's third law (Kepler's Law of time periods):
$T^{2} \propto r^{3}$
where $\mathrm{T}=$ Time period of revolution
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{r}} = $ Semi - major axis
$\left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{3} \Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{24}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{r}{4 \times r}\right)^{3}$
$\dfrac{T_{2}^{2}}{24 \times 24}=\dfrac{1}{4 \times 4 \times 4}$
$T_{2}^{2}=\dfrac{24 \times 24}{4 \times 4 \times 4}=\dfrac{6 \times 6}{4} \Rightarrow T_{2}=\dfrac{6}{2}=3 h r s$
subject: Physics

question: If the distance of the earth from sun is $1.5 \times {10^8}\,km$, then the distance of an imaginary planet from Sun, if its period of revolution is 2.83 years is
explanation:
From Kepler's law of time period,
$T \propto R^{3 / 2}$ or $T^{2} \propto R^{3}$
Now, $\left(\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}\right)^{3}$
Here $T_{1}=365$ days $=1$ year
$\Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{1 \text { year }}{2.83 \text { years }}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{6}}{R_{2}}\right)^{3}$
$ \Rightarrow {R_2} = 3 \times {10^6}\,km$
So, correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The angular speed of the moon in its orbit about the earth is more than the angular speed of the earth in its orbit about the sun.

Reason: The moon takes less time to move around the earth than the time taken by the earth to move around the sun.
explanation:
$T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega} \quad \Rightarrow \omega=\dfrac{2 \pi}{T}$
$\therefore \omega \alpha \dfrac{1}{T}, \quad \dfrac{\omega_{M}}{\omega_{E}}=\dfrac{T_{E}}{T_{M}}$
So correct option is (1)

question: The distance of Neptune and Saturn from the Sun are respectively $10^{13}$ and $10^{12}$ meters and their periodic times are respectively $T_{n}$ and $T_{s}$. If their orbits are assumed to be circular, the value of $T_{n} / T_{s}$ is

explanation:

question: A planet is revolving around the sun in a circular orbit with a radius $r$. The time period is $T$. If the force between the planet and star is proportional to $r^{-3 / 2}$, then the square of time period is proportioanl to

question: The distance between Sun and Earth is $R$. The duration of year if the distance between Sun and Earth becomes 3$R$ will be :

$T=$ 1 year
$\Rightarrow T^{\prime}=3 \sqrt{3}$ years

question: A planet is revolving around sun in elliptical orbit. Maximum & minimum distance of planet from sun are respectively $1.6 \times {10^{12}}\,m$ and $6 \times {10^{10}}\,m$. Find minimum speed if maximum speed of planet is $8 \times {10^4}\,m/s$ ?
explanation:
From conservation of angular momentum
$L = m{v_{\max }}{r_{\min }} = m{v_{\min {\rm{ }}}} \times {r_{\max }}$
$\therefore 8 \times {10^4} \times 6 \times {10^{10}} = {v_{\min {\rm{ }}}} \times 1.6 \times {10^{12}}$
$\therefore {v_{\min {\rm{ }}}} = 3 \times {10^3}\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: If the angular momentum of a planet of mass $m$, moving around the Sun in a circular orbit is $L$, about the centre of the Sun, its areal velocity is
explanation:
The angular momentum of the planet about the sun is $\vec L = \vec r \times m\vec r$
$L = rm\frac{{\Delta s}}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{m}{{\Delta t}}(r\Delta s)$
$L = \frac{m}{{\Delta t}}(2\Delta A) \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta A}}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{L}{{2\,m}}$
Where ${\Delta A}$ is the area sweep by the planet in time ${\Delta t}$.
subject: Physics

question: Figure shows elliptical path abcd of a planet around the sun S such that the area of triangle csa is $\dfrac{1}{4}$ the area of the ellipse . (See figure) With $db$ as the semi major axis, and $ca$ as the semi minor axis. If $t_{1}$ is the time taken for planet to go over path $abc$ and $t_{2}$ for path taken over $cda$ then :

https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951458/preptracker/questions/q_35268_explanation_1764951457588.png For the given elliptical path.
Area of $sabc = \frac{{3A}}{4}$
Area of $s a d c=\dfrac{A}{4}($ Given in the problem $)$
Area of $s a b c=$ Area of $a b c+$ Area of $c s a$
$ = \frac{A}{2} + \frac{A}{4} = \frac{{3\,A}}{4}$
From Kepler's $2^{\text {nd }}$ law
$\frac{{\Delta A}}{{\Delta t}} = $ constant
$\frac{{3\,A}}{{4{t_1}}} = \frac{A}{{4{t_2}}} \Rightarrow {t_1} = 3{t_2}$
subject: Physics

question: India's Mangalyan was sent to the Mars by launching it into a transfer orbit $EOM$ around the sun. It leaves the earth at $E$ and meets Mars at $M$. If the semi-major axis of Earth's orbit is ${a_e} = 1.5 \times {10^{11}}\,m$, that of Mar's orbit ${a_m} = 2.28 \times {10^{11}}\,m$, taken Kepler's laws give the estimate of time for Manglyan to reach Mars from Earth to be close to :
explanation:
When the mangalyan travels from earth to mars we assume that it travels in elliptical path and it covers exactly half of the new ellipse. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951453/preptracker/questions/q_35248_explanation_1764951452530.png Let $r$ is the length of semi major axis of the ellipse
$\begin{aligned}& \stackrel{\leftrightarrow a_{m} \longrightarrow a_{e}}{\stackrel{\leftarrow}{\rightleftarrows}} \\& r=\dfrac{a_{m}+a_{e}}{2}=\dfrac{1.5+2.28}{2}=1.89\end{aligned}$
We know that the time period T is ${T^2}\alpha \,{r^3}$
$\left(\dfrac{T_{E}}{T}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{r_{E}}{r}\right)^{3}, T=T_{E}\left(\dfrac{r}{r_{E}}\right)^{3 / 2}$
Time taken to travel from $E$ to $\mathrm{M}$ is
$\begin{aligned}& t_{E M}=\dfrac{T}{2}=\dfrac{T_{E}}{2}\left(\dfrac{r}{r_{E}}\right)^{3 / 2}=\dfrac{365}{2}\left(\dfrac{1.89}{1.5}\right)^{3 / 2} \\& t_{E M}=257.3 \text { days. }\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: If a graph is plotted between $T^{2}$ and $r^{3}$ for a planet, then its slope will be (where $M_{s}$ is the mass of the sun)
explanation:
Time period of a revolution of a planet
$T=\dfrac{2 \pi r}{v}=\dfrac{2 \pi r}{\sqrt{\dfrac{G M_{s}}{r}}}=\dfrac{2 \pi r^{3 / 2}}{\sqrt{G M_{s}}}$
Where $M_{s}$ is the mass of the sun
Squaring both sides, we get
$T^{2}=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} r^{3}}{G M_{s}}$
The graph between $T^{2}$ and $r^{3}$ is a straight line whose slope is $\dfrac{4 \pi^{2}}{G M_{s}}$.
subject: Physics

question: Which one of the following quantities does not depend upon the orbital radius of the satellite.
explanation:
From kepler's law
$T^{2} \alpha R^{3} \Rightarrow \dfrac{T^{2}}{R^{3}}=\text { constant }$
subject: Physics

question: A planet moves in a circular path. It describes equal angles about the centre in equal intervals of time. Which of the following statements about the velocity of the planet is true?
explanation: As body covers equal angles in equal time intervals of time, so magnitude of linear velocity is constant.

Magnitude of velocity is not constant
Both magnitude and direction of velocity change
Magnitude of velocity is constant but direction changes

question: A planet moving along an elliptical orbit is close to the sun at a distance $r_{1}$ and farthest away at a distance of $r_{2}$. If $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ are the linear velocities at these points respectively. Then the ratio $\frac{{{v_1}}}{{{v_2}}}$  is
explanation:
From conservation of angular momentum of the planet of mass $m$.

explanation:
Time period is related with radius as
$\begin{aligned}& T^{2} \alpha r^{3} \\& \left(\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)^{3} \\& \left(\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{r_{1}}{\left(r_{1} / 4\right)}\right)^{3}=4^{3} \\& \dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=4^{3 / 2}=8 \Rightarrow T_{2}=\dfrac{T_{1}}{8}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

Different at different points of the orbit

explanation:
From Kepler's $2^{\text {nd }}$ law
$\frac{{\Delta A}}{{\Delta t}} = $ constant
$\frac{{\Delta {A_1}}}{{\Delta {A_2}}} = \frac{{\Delta {t_1}}}{{\Delta {t_2}}}$
$\frac{A}{{A'}} = \frac{{7\,\operatorname{days} }}{{21\,\operatorname{days} }}$
$A' = 3A$

question: According to kepler's second law, line joining the planet to the sun sweeps out equal areas in equal time intervals. This suggests that for the planet.

question: In planetary motion the areal velocity of position vector of a planet depends on angular velocity$(\omega)$ and the distance of the planet from sun ($r$). The correct relation for areal velocity is
explanation:
The areal velocity is
$\dfrac{d A}{d t}=\dfrac{L}{2 m} \Rightarrow \dfrac{d A}{d t} \alpha \omega r^{2}$
