question: The radius vector drawn from the sun to a planet sweeps out ______areas in equal time
subject: Physics

question: Suppose the law of gravitational attraction suddenly changes and becomes an inverse cube law i.e. $F \alpha \dfrac{1}{r^{3}}$, but still remaining a central force. Then
explanation:
Given that
$\begin{aligned}& F=\dfrac{k}{r^{3}}=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r} \\& v^{2}=\dfrac{k}{m r^{2}} \Rightarrow v=\dfrac{c}{r}=\dfrac{2 \pi r}{T} \\& \Rightarrow r^{2} \alpha T\end{aligned}$
So Kepler's law of period does not hold.
Kepler's law of area is derived from conservation of angular momentum. Angular moment is conserved for central force. So kepler's law of area still holds.

options.text: Kepler's law of period still holds
Kepler's law of area still holds
Neither the law of area nor the law of period still holds
Kepler's law of area and period still holds

subject: Physics

question: The earth moves around the Sun in an elliptical orbit as shown in the figure. The ratio $OA/OB = x$. The ratio of the speed of the earth at $B$ to that at $A$ is nearly.
explanation:
Applying conservation of angular momentum at positions $A$ and $B$
$m v_{A} \times O A=m v_{B} \times O B$
Hence, $\quad \dfrac{v_{B}}{v_{A}}=\dfrac{O A}{O B}=x$
subject: Physics

question: A planet is revolving around the sun as shown in elliptical path. The correct option is
explanation: During path $D A B$ planet is nearer to sun as comparing with path $BCD$. From conservation of angular momentum $v r=$ constant. So time taken in travelling $DAB$ is less than that for $BCD$ because velocity of planet will be more in region $DAB$

options.text: The time taken in travelling $CDA$ is greater than that for $ABC$
The time taken in travelling $DAB$ is less than that for $BCD$
The time taken in travelling $DAB$ is greater than that for $BCD$
The time taken in travelling $CDA$ is less than that for $ABC$

subject: Physics

question: A planet of mass $m$ moves along an elliptical path around sun so that perihelion and aphelion distances are $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$. Find the angular momentum of the planet.
explanation:
From conservation of angular momentum
$m v_{1} r_{1}=m v_{2} r_{2}$
Using energy conservation
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{-G m M_{S}}{r_{1}}+\dfrac{m v_{1}^{2}}{2}=\dfrac{-G m M_{S}}{r_{2}}+\dfrac{m v_{2}^{2}}{2} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{-G M_{S}}{r_{1}}+\dfrac{G M_{S}}{r_{2}}=\dfrac{v_{2}^{2}}{2}-\dfrac{v_{2}^{2}}{2}\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{2} \\& \Rightarrow v_{2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M_{S} r_{1}}{r_{2}\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right)}}\end{aligned}$
and $L=m v_{2} r_{2}=m \sqrt{\dfrac{2 G M_{S} r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}}$
subject: Physics

question: A saturn year is 29.5 times the earth year. How far is the saturn from the Sun if the earth is $1.5 \times {10^8}\,km$ away from the Sun?
explanation:
Here,
${T_S} = 29.5\,{T_E},\,{R_E} = 1.5 \times {10^8}km,\,{R_s} = ?$
According to Kepler's third law, $T^{2} \propto R^{3}$
$\therefore \,\frac{{T_S^2}}{{T_E^2}} = \frac{{R_S^3}}{{R_E^3}}$
${R_S} = {R_E}{\left( {\frac{{{T_S}}}{{{T_E}}}} \right)^{2/3}}$
${R_S} = 1.5 \times {10^8}{\left( {\frac{{29.5{T_E}}}{{{T_E}}}} \right)^{2/3}} = 1.4 \times {10^9}km$
subject: Physics

question: A particle is moving with a uniform speed in a circular orbit of radius $R$ in a central force inversely proportional to the $n^{\text {th }}$ power of $R$. If the period of rotation of the particle is $T$, then,
explanation:
Centripetal force
$F = m{\omega ^2}R\alpha \frac{1}{{{R^n}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\omega ^2} \propto \frac{1}{{{R^{n + 1}}}} \Rightarrow \omega \propto \frac{1}{{{R^{\frac{{n + 1}}{2}}}}}$
Time period $T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega } \propto {R^{\frac{{n + 1}}{2}}}$
subject: Physics

question: In planetary motion the areal velocity of position vector of a planet depends on angular velocity $(\omega)$ and the distance of the planet from sun ($r$). If so the correct relation for areal velocity is
explanation:
The areal velocity is
$\frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = \frac{L}{{2m}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dA}}{{dt}} \propto vr \propto \omega {r^2}$
subject: Physics

question: Kepler's third law states that square of period of revolution ($T$) of a planet around the sun is proportional to third power of average distance $r$ between sun and planet i.e., ${T^2} = K{r^3}$, here $K$ is constant. If the masses of sun and planet are $M$ and $m$ respectively, then as per Newton's law of gravitation the force of attraction between them is $F=\dfrac{G M m}{r^{2}}$, here $G$ is gravitational constant. The relation between $G$ and $K$ is described as:
subject: Physics

question: The largest and the shortest distance of the earth from the sun are $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$, its distance from the sun when it is perpendicular to the major axis of the orbit drawn from the sun, is
explanation:
The earth moves around the sun in elliptical path as shown in the figure. So by using the properties of ellipse https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951254/preptracker/questions/q_34552_explanation_1764951254197.png $r_{1}=(1+e) a \text { and } r_{2}=(1-e) a$
where, $a=$ length of semi-major axis
$b=$ length of semi-major axis
$e=$ eccentricity
Given that
$\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{r_{1}+r_{2}}{2}$
and $r_{1} r_{2}=\left(1-e^{2}\right) a^{2}$
Now, required distance $=$ semi latusrectum
$\begin{aligned}F_{1} P & =\dfrac{b^{2}}{a} \\& =\dfrac{a^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right)}{a}=\dfrac{r_{1} r_{2}}{\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right) / 2}=\dfrac{2 r_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The figure shows elliptical orbit of a planet $m$ about the sun $S$. The shaded area $SCD$ is twice the shaded area $SAB$. If $t_{1}$ is the time for the planet to move from $C$ to $D$ and $t_{2}$ is the time to move from $A$ to $B$, then:
explanation:
Applying Kepler's law of area of planetary motion. The line joining the sun to the planet sweeps out equal areas in equal time interval i.e., areal velocity is constant
$\dfrac{d A}{d t}=\text { constant } \text { or } \dfrac{A_{1}}{t_{1}}=\dfrac{A_{2}}{t_{2}} \Rightarrow t_{1}=\dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}} t_{2}$
Given $A_{1}=2 A_{2}$
$\therefore t_{1}=2 t_{2}$
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following orbits is a possible orbit for a planet $(P)$ ?
explanation:
According to Kepler's first law, every planet moves in an elliptical orbit with the sun situated at one of the foci of the ellipse.
In options (1) and (2) sun is not at a focus while in (4) The planet is not in orbit around the sun. Only option (3) Represents the possible orbit for planet.

explanation: Asteroids move in circular orbits like planets and they obey Keplar's laws.

question: The kinetic energies of a planet in an elliptical orbit about the Sun, at positions $A$, $B$ and $C$ are $K_{A}, K_{B}$ and $K_{C}$ respectively. $AC$ is the major axis and $SB$ is perpendicular to $AC$ at the position of the Sun $S$ is shown in the figure. Then,

question: A satellite of mass $m$ is revolving in circular orbit of radius $r$ around the earth. Its angular momentum w.r.t the centre of its orbit is ($M=$ mass of earth, $G=$ universal gravitational constant)
explanation:
Orbital speed of the statellite,$v=\sqrt{\dfrac{G M}{r}}$
Angular momentum of the statellite,
$L=m v r=m r \times \sqrt{\dfrac{G M}{r}}=\left(G M m^{2} r\right)^{1 / 2}$
subject: Physics

question: A planet moving around sun sweeps area $A_{1}$ in 2 days, $A_{2}$ in 3 days and $A_{3}$ in 6 days. Then the relation between $A_{1}, A_{2}$ and $A_{3}$ is
explanation:
When a planet revolves around the sun, its area velocity is constant
$\therefore \frac{{{A_1}}}{{{t_1}}} = \frac{{{A_2}}}{{{t_2}}} = \frac{{{A_3}}}{{{t_3}}}$
$\frac{{{A_1}}}{2} = \frac{{{A_2}}}{3} = \frac{{{A_3}}}{6} \Rightarrow 3\,{A_1} = 2\,{A_2} = {A_3}$

$\therefore {v_2} = \frac{{{d_1}{v_1}}}{{\,{d_2}}}$

question: A satellite in a circular orbit of radius $R$ has period of 4 hours. Another satellite with orbital radius $3 R$ around the same planet will have a period (in hours)
explanation:
From Kepler's third law, $T^{2} \propto R^{3}$
$\therefore \frac{{{T_1}}}{{\,{T_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{R}{{3R}}} \right)^{3/2}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{T_1}}}{{\,{T_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right)^{3/2}}$
$\,{T_2} = {T_1}{(3)^{3/2}} = 4\sqrt {27} {\rm{ }}$ hours

question: Match the following scientists with their contributions to the development of theories related to gravitation and choose the correct option from the codes given below:
explanation:
A. Galileo observed that all bodies are accelerated towards the Earth with a constant acceleration, which is now known as the acceleration due to gravity (Q).
B. Ptolemy's model placed the Earth at the center of the universe with celestial bodies, including the Sun, revolving around it (R).
C. Copernicus challenged the geocentric model with his heliocentric model, which correctly placed the Sun at the center of the universe with the Earth and other planets revolving around it S.
D. Newton built on the work of his predecessors to propose the universal law of gravitation, which describes the force of attraction between masses (S). Correct option is (4).

options.text:
A - P,
 B - Q,
 C - R,
 D - S
A - R, B - P, C - Q, D - S

A - S,
 B - R,
 C - P,
 D - Q
A - Q, B - R, C - S, D - P

subject: Physics

question: Gravitational forces operate among which of the following?
explanation: Gravitational forces operate among all objects in the universe. All object having mass are affected by gravitational forces exerted by another objects having mass. It has to be noted that photon are massless particles (quanta of light) yet recent discoveries of gravitational waves and lensing showed that light (photons) is also affected by gravitational forces. So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: Find the acceleration of our galaxy, due to the nearest comparably sized galaxy. The approximate masses of each galaxy is $8 \times 10^{11}$ solar mass and they are separated by 2 million light-years. Each galaxy has a diameter of 100000 light year.(Assume, 1 light year $\approx 10^{16} {~m}$, gravitational constant, $G \approx 10^{-10}\left(\dfrac{N m^{2}}{k g^{2}}\right)$ and mass of the sun $=2.0 \times 10^{30} {~kg}$)
explanation:
Force of gravitational pull on each of galaxy is
$F=\dfrac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{d^{2}}=\dfrac{10^{-10} \times\left(16 \times 10^{41}\right)^{2}}{\left(2 \times 10^{22}\right)^{2}} N$
Acceleration of galaxy is
$a=\dfrac{F}{m}=\dfrac{10^{-10} \times\left(16 \times 10^{41}\right)^{2}}{\left(2 \times 10^{22}\right)^{2} \times 16 \times 10^{41}} {~ms}^{-2}$
$=\dfrac{10^{-10} \times 16 \times 10^{41}}{\left(2 \times 10^{22}\right)^{2}}=4 \times 10^{-13} {~ms}^{-2}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A projectile of mass ${m}$ is fired from the surface of earth at an angle ${\alpha=60^{\circ}}$ from the vertical. The initial speed of the projectile is ${u=\sqrt{\dfrac{G M}{R}}}$, where ${M}$ and ${R}$ are mass and radius of the earth. Find the height by the projectile in kilometer. Neglect air resistance and the rotation of earth. (Given ${R=6400 {~km}}$)
explanation:
Let ${v}$ be the speed of projectile at the highest point. By conservation of angular momentum and conservation of mechanical energy, we have
${m u R \sin \alpha=m v r_{\max }}$ and ${\dfrac{1}{2} m u^{2}-\dfrac{G M m}{R}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\dfrac{G M m}{r_{\max }}}$
Solving these two equations, we get ${r_{\max }=\dfrac{3 R}{2}}$
${\therefore \quad h_{\text {max }}=r_{\text {max }}-R=\dfrac{R}{2}=3200 {~km}}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle of mass ${m}$ is projected from the surface of the earth with velocity ${10 {~km} / {sec}}$ at an angle ${60^{\circ}}$ with horizontal. If ${V}$ is the speed of the particle at the point of maximum height of the particle from the surface of the earth, then find the value of ${V}$ which will be less than (a minimum value of velocity in ${{km} / {sec}}$ ) an integer from 0 to 9.
explanation:
Conservation of momentum at ${P}$ and ${Q\left(m v_{0} \cos \theta\right) R=}$
$mv(R + h)v = \left( {\frac{R}{{R + h}}} \right){v_0}\cos \theta \,v < {v_0} = \cos \theta $https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955940/preptracker/questions/q_36162_explanation_1764955940437.png or ${\quad v < (10)\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)}$
or, ${\quad v < 5 {~km} / {sec}}$
subject: Physics

question: If a projectile has a velocity greater than the escape velocity, which trajectory will it follow?
explanation:
If a projectile has velocity greater than the escape velocity, then the body is able to overcome the gravitational field and the body will move in space with in hyperbolic trajectory i.e., unbound motion as ${E>0}$.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: At what temperature, hydrogen molecules will escape from the earth's surface? (Take, radius of earth  $ = 6.4 \times {10^6}\,m,$ mass of hydrogen molecule $ = 0.34 \times {10^{ - 26}}\,kg,$ Boltzmann constant $ = 1.38 \times {10^{ - 23}}J{K^{ - 1}}$ and acceleration due to gravity $ = 9.8\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
The root mean square velocity of gas is
${v_{rms}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3\,kT}}{m}} \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Escape velocity of the gas molecules is
${v_e} = \sqrt {2\,g{R_e}} \quad \quad \quad (2)$
As the root mean square velocity of gas molecules must be equal to the escape velocity.
$\therefore$ From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\sqrt {\frac{{3kT}}{m}} = \sqrt {2g{R_e}} $
$ \Rightarrow T = \frac{{2g{R_e}m}}{{3k}}$
$ \Rightarrow T = \frac{{2 \times 9.8 \times 6.4 \times {{10}^6} \times 0.34 \times {{10}^{ - 26}}}}{{3\left( {1.38 \times {{10}^{ - 23}}} \right)}}$
$ = {10^4}\,K$
Therefore, ${10^4}\,K$ is the temperature at which hydrogen molecules will escape from earth's surface.
subject: Physics

question: A space rocket is intended to leave the earth's gravitational field. The fuel in its main engine is a little less than the amount that is necessary and an auxiliary engine, (only capable of operating for a short time) has to be used as well. When it is best to switch ON, the auxiliary engine
explanation: For a space rocket, most of its thrust is required during take OFF. The rocket combustion causes fuel to burn and result in decrease of weight, which causes further acceleration using less fuel. So it is best to switch ON the auxiliary engine at take OFF.

options.text: when the rocket has nearly stopped with respect to the earth.

subject: Physics

question: If there was a reduction in gravitational effect, then which of the following forces do you think would change in some respect?
explanation: Magnetic, electrostatic and viscous force will not be affected by reduction in gravitational force. Only Archimedes uplift (which is loss in weight equal to the weight of liquid displaced by the immersed part of body) will change.

question: For a body projected at an angle with the horizontal from the ground, choose the correct statement.
explanation:
(1),(2) At the highest point of the projectile, horizontal component of velocity is not zero, so $K . E$ is not zero.
(3) As vertical component of velocity is zero at highest point, vertical component of momentum is zero.
(4) Gravitational potential energy depends on height.

options.text: The kinetic energy (KE) is zero at the highest point of projectile motion.
The horizontal component of velocity is zero at the highest point.
The vertical component of momentum is maximum at the highest point.
Gravitational potential energy is maximum at the highest point.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The length (i.e, duration) of the day is slowly increasing, due to rotational effect.

Reason: The dominant effect causing a slowdown in the rotation of the earth is the gravitational pull of other planets in the solar system.
explanation: $T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}, T$ depends on $\omega$. The length (duration of) the day is indeed increasing, but the primary cause is the transfer of angular momentum between the Earth and the Moon due to tidal forces, not the gravitational pull of other planets. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The earth without its atmosphere would be inhospitably cold.

Reason: All heat would escape in the absence of atmosphere.
explanation: The Earth's atmosphere helps to retain heat through the greenhouse effect. Heat radiated out by earth is reflected back by the atmosphere without it, the surface would be much colder as all heat escapes. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Planet is a heavenly body revolving around the sun.

Reason: Star is luminous body made of gaseous material.
explanation: A celestial object that orbits the sun is referred to as a planet, as in our solar system. On the other hand, a celestial body composed of gaseous material and emitting its own light due to internal energy sources is known as a star. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Earth is flat at the poles.

Reason: This is due to rotation of earth about its axis.
explanation: The Earth, is slightly flattened at the poles and bulging at the equator. Rotation of earth creates a centrifugal force. So correct option is (1).

question:
Assertion: The motion of a particle under the central force is always confined to a plane.

Reason: Angular momentum is always conserved in the motion under a central force.
explanation:
$\vec{\tau}=\dfrac{d \vec{L}}{d t}=[\vec{r} \times \vec{F}(r)]$
In a central force field $\vec{F}(r)$, the rotational motion (caused by $\vec{\tau}$ ) of a particle is always confined to a plane due to the conservation of angular momentum as $F$ is function of $r$ only. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: The ratio of inertial mass to gravitational mass is equal to one.

Reason: The inertial mass and gravitational mass of a body are conceptually different.
explanation: Inertial mass $\left(m_{0}\right)$ indicates object's resistance to change its velocity. But, gravitational mass $(m)$ is in universal law. $m_{0}$ is in Newton's law $F=m_{0} a$ where $a=\dfrac{d v}{d t}$. But, $m$ is in formula $F=m g=\dfrac{G M_{e} m}{R_{E}^{2}}$ (written here on earth of mass $M_{e}$ ). At a given place, $\mathrm{m}$ and $m_{0}$ are taken to be equivalent. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: A point $P(\sqrt{3} R, 0,0)$ lies on the axis of a ring of a mass ' $M$ ' and radius ' $R$ '. The ring is located in $y$-z plane with its centre at origin ' $O$ '. A small particle of mass ' $m$ ' starts from ' $P$ ' and reaches ' $O$ ' under gravitational attraction only. Its speed ' $O$ ' will be
explanation:
According to law of conservation of energy
$\dfrac{1}{2} m V^{2}=m(\Delta V)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m V^{2}=m\left[\dfrac{-G M}{2 R}-\left(-\dfrac{G M}{R}\right)\right]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m V^{2}=m\left(\dfrac{G M}{2 R}\right) \Rightarrow V=\sqrt{\dfrac{G M}{R}}$.

explanation:

$g=\dfrac{G M_{e}}{R_{e}^{2}}$
$M_{e}=\dfrac{g R_{e}^{2}}{G}$

question: Different points in earth are at slightly different distances from the sun and hence experience different forces due to gravitation. For a rigid body, we know that if various forces act at various points in it, the resultant motion is as if a net force acts on the $CM$ (centre of mass) causing translation and a net torque at the $CM$ causing rotation around an axis through the $CM$. For the earth-sun system (approximating the earth as a uniform density sphere)
explanation:
The earth is revolving in circular orbit around sun due to gravitational force $(F)$ which act along the radius of circular path, towards the sun. As torque
$=|\tau|=|r \times F|=r F \sin 0^{\circ}=0$
Therefore, torque is zero.

question: How will you weigh of sun i.e., estimate its mass? You will need to know the period of one of its planets and the radius of the planetary orbit. The mean orbital radius of the earth around the sun is $1.5 \times {10^8}\,km$. Then the mass of the sun is
explanation:
Mean orbital radius of the earth around the sun
$r = 1.5 \times {10^8}\,km = 1.5 \times {10^{11}}\,m$
Time period of earth around the sun $ = 1yr$
$=$365 days $ = 365 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60\,s$
The orbital velocity is
${v^2} = \frac{{G{M_s}}}{r}$
$\therefore \quad {\left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{T}} \right)^2} = \frac{{G{M_s}}}{{{r^3}}}\quad \left( {v = \frac{{2\pi r}}{T}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {M_s} = \frac{{4{\pi ^2}{r^3}}}{{G{T^2}}}$
$ = \frac{{4 \times {{(3.14)}^2} \times {{(1.5 \times {{10}^{11}})}^3}}}{{6.67 \times {{10}^{ - 11}} \times {{(365 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60)}^2}}} = 2 \times {10^{30}}kg$

question:
Two bodies of masses 8 $kg$ are placed at the vertices $A$ and $B$ of an equilateral triangle $A B C$.
A third body of mass 2 $kg$ is placed at the centroid $G$ of the triangle. If $AG = BG = CG = 1\,m$, where should a fourth body of mass 4 $kg$ be placed so that the resultant force on the 2 $kg$ body is zero?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958069/preptracker/questions/q_109781_explanation_1764958069133.png From the geometry of the problem, ${F_{GA}} = {F_{GB}} = \frac{{G \cdot 8 \cdot 2}}{{{1^2}}} = 16KN$
From symmetry, net resultant force acting vertically downwards
$F = 2 \times 16K \times \cos \left( {\frac{{120^\circ }}{2}} \right) = 16K\,N$
$\therefore$ The resultant force on the mass at $G$ will be zero if a mass of 4 $kg$ is placed at $P(GP = d)$ so that $F_{G P}=F$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{K \cdot 4 \cdot 2}{d^{2}}=16 K \Rightarrow d=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} m$.

options.text: At a point $P$ on the line $C G$ such that $P G=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} m$
At a point $P$ on the line $CG$ such that $PG = 0.5\,m$
At a point $P$ on the line $CG$ such that $P G=2 m$

question: Two stars of masses '$M$' and '$2 M$' that are at a distance '$d$' apart, are revolving one around another. The angular velocity of the system of two stars is ($G$ - Universal gravitational constant)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958067/preptracker/questions/q_109776_explanation_1764958066512.png For star of mass $M$,
$F_{g}=M \omega^{2} d_{1}$ where $d_{1}=\dfrac{2 d}{3}$
$d_{1}=\dfrac{M \times 0+2 M \times d}{M+2 M}=\dfrac{2}{3} d$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{G(M)(2 M)}{d^{2}}=M \omega^{2}\left(\dfrac{2 d}{3}\right)$
$\therefore \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{3 G M}{d^{3}}}$

question: A point mass $m$ is located at a distance $r$ from a uniform thin rod of mass $M$ and length $L$ as shown in the figure. The magnitude of the gravitational force of attraction is
explanation:
The rod - particle system is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958063/preptracker/questions/q_109701_explanation_1764958063003.png Let $d m$ be the mass of element $d x$ at distance $x$ from right end of the rod.
Now, mass of element $d x, d m=\dfrac{M}{L} d x$
The separation between point mass and element will be $(r+x)$.
As we know that, $F=\dfrac{G M_{1} M_{2}}{d^{2}}$
$\therefore d F($ elemental force $)=\dfrac{G d m \cdot m}{(r+x)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \int_{0}^{F} d F=\int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{(G M / L) d x \cdot m}{(r+x)^{2}}$
$F=\dfrac{G M m}{L} \int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{d x}{(r+x)^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{G M m}{L} \cdot\left[-(r+x)^{-1}\right]_{0}^{L}$
$=-\dfrac{G M m}{L}\left[\dfrac{1}{r+L}-\dfrac{1}{r}\right]$
$=-\dfrac{G M m}{L}\left[\dfrac{r-r-L}{r(r+L)}\right]=\dfrac{G M m}{r(r+L)}$
Magnitude of gravitational force, $F=\dfrac{G M m}{r(r+L)}$
So, the correct option is (3).

question: Solid sphere of mass $M$, radius $R$ exerts force $F$ on a point mass. Now a concentric spherical mass $\dfrac{M}{7}$ is removed. What is new force?
explanation:
Force due to solid sphere of mass $M$ and radius $R$ on point mass $m$ at distance $r$ is:
$F=\dfrac{G M m}{r^{2}}$https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958055/preptracker/questions/q_109675_explanation_1764958055118.png After concentric spherical mass is removed, mass of the remaining portion is :
$M^{\prime}=M-\dfrac{M}{7}=\dfrac{6 M}{7}$
So, force due to remaining portion is :
$F^{\prime}=\dfrac{G M^{\prime} m}{r^{2}}=\dfrac{G}{r^{2}}\left(\dfrac{6 M}{7}\right) m=\dfrac{6}{7}\left(\dfrac{G M m}{r^{2}}\right)$
$F^{\prime}=\dfrac{6}{7} F$
So, correct answer is (2).

question: Four identical particles of mass $m$ are kept at the four corners of a square. If the gravitational force exerted on one of the masses by the other masses is $\left(\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2}+1}{32}\right) \dfrac{G m^{2}}{L^{2}}$, the length of the sides of the square is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951664/preptracker/questions/q_36267_explanation_1764951664047.png
The force on one mass by other masses
$F_{\text {Net }}=\dfrac{G m^{2}}{2 x^{2}}+\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{G m^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\dfrac{G m^{2}}{x^{2}}\right)^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{G m^{2}}{2 x^{2}}+\dfrac{G m^{2} \sqrt{2}}{x^{2}}=\dfrac{G m^{2}}{x^{2}}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}+\sqrt{2}\right)$
On comparing with the given value of force,
$x^{2}=16 L^{2} \Rightarrow x=4 L$

question: A metal wire of uniform mass density having length $L$ and mass $M$ is bent to form a semicircular arc and a particle of mass $m$ is placed at the centre of the arc. The gravitational force on the particle by the wire is
explanation:
$L=\pi R ; \quad R=\dfrac{L}{\pi}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951624/preptracker/questions/q_36260_explanation_1764951623845.png
Consider an elementary portion of length $d l$.
Linear mass density is given by $\lambda=\dfrac{M}{L}$;
$d l=R d \theta$
Mass of element is $d M=\lambda d l=\dfrac{M}{L} R d \theta$
Force due to elementary portion
$d F=\dfrac{G m \cdot \dfrac{M}{L} \cdot R d \theta}{R^{2}}$ along $O P$
Resolving the components,
$d F_{x}=d F \cos \theta=\dfrac{G m M}{L R} \cos \theta d \theta$
$d F_{y}=d F \sin \theta=\dfrac{G m M \sin \theta d \theta}{L R}$
$F_{x}=\dfrac{G m M}{L R} \int_{0}^{\pi} \cos \theta d \theta=\dfrac{G m M}{L R}[\sin \theta]_{0}^{\pi}=0$
$F_{y}=\dfrac{G m M}{L R} \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin \theta d \theta=\dfrac{G m M}{L R}[-\cos \theta]_{0}^{\pi}$
$\therefore$ Net force at the mass $m$ is
$F = {F_y} = \frac{{GmM}}{{LR}} \times 2 = $
$\frac{{GmM \times 2 \times \pi }}{{L \times L}} = \frac{{2GmM\pi }}{{{L^2}}}$

question: Four identical particles of mass $M$ are located at the corners of a square of side ' $a$ '. If each of them revolves under the influence of others gravitational field in a circular orbit circumscribing the square, their speed is found to be $Q$ times $\sqrt {\frac{{GM}}{a}} .$ The value of $Q$ is
explanation:
Let ${F_1},{\rm{ }}{F_2}$ and ${F_3}$ be the forces acting on particle at $A$ due to particles at $B\,\,C$ and $D$ respectively.
Net force on $A$,
${F_A} = {F_1}\cos 45^\circ + {F_2}\cos 45^\circ + {F_3}$ [along $x$$ - $direction] https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951636/preptracker/questions/q_36118_explanation_1764951635619.png The $y$ $ - $ components of $F_{1}$ and $F_{2}$ cancels each other. Net force on A provides the centripetal force.
$\therefore \quad \frac{{M{v^2}}}{r} = 2\,{F_1}\cos 45^\circ + {F_3}$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
for value of $r$, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951636/preptracker/questions/q_36118_explanation_1764951636412.png From geometry $r^{2}+r^{2}=a^{2}$
$ \Rightarrow r = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}\quad \quad (2)$
putting (2) in (1)
$\frac{{M{v^2}}}{{\frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}}} = \frac{{2GMM}}{{{a^2}}}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} + \frac{{GMM}}{{2{a^2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {v^2} = \frac{{GM}}{a}\left( {1 + \frac{1}{{2\sqrt 2 }}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow {v^2} = \frac{{2\sqrt 2 + 1}}{{2\sqrt 2 }}\frac{{GM}}{a}$
$ \Rightarrow v = \sqrt {\frac{{3.82}}{{2.82}}} \sqrt {\frac{{GM}}{a}} = 1.16\sqrt {\frac{{GM}}{a}} $

question: A uniform ring of mass $2 m$ and radius ' $a$ ' is placed directly above a uniform sphere of mass $M$ and of equal radius. The centre of ring is at distance $\sqrt 3 a$ from the centre of sphere. The gravitational force exerted by the sphere on the ring is $N\frac{{GMm}}{{{a^2}}}$ units. What is the value of  $N$ ?
explanation:
$F = G\frac{{Mdm}}{{4{a^2}}}$
$F = \sum {\rm{ }} dF\cos \theta $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951626/preptracker/questions/q_36062_explanation_1764951625348.png $ = \sum {\rm{ }} \frac{{GMdm}}{{4{a^2}}}\cos \theta $
$ = \frac{{GM}}{{4{a^2}}} \cdot \frac{{\sqrt {3a} }}{{2a}}\sum {dm} $
$ = \frac{{\sqrt 3 GM}}{{8{a^2}}} \cdot 2\,m$
$ = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{4} \cdot \frac{{GMm}}{{{a^2}}}$
$\therefore F = 0.43\frac{{GMm}}{{{a^2}}}$
$\therefore N = 0.43$

question: Three equal masses of $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ each are placed at the vertices of an equilateral $\triangle P Q R$ and a mass of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is placed at the centroid $O$ of the triangle which is at a distance of $\sqrt{2} \mathrm{~m}$ from each of the vertices of the triangle. The force, (in newton) acting on the mass of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is
explanation:
Given, $O P=O Q=O R=\sqrt{2} m$ (given) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951605/preptracker/questions/q_35917_explanation_1764951604615.png The gravitational force on mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ due to mass $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ at $P$,
$F_{O P}=G \dfrac{2 \times 1}{(\sqrt{2})^{2}}(\text { along } O P)$
Similarly, $F_{O Q}=G \dfrac{2 \times 1}{(\sqrt{2})^{2}}$ (along $O Q$ )
and $\quad F_{O R}=G \dfrac{2 \times 1}{(\sqrt{2})^{2}}$ (along $O R$ )
$F_{O Q} \cos 30^{\circ}$ and $F_{O R} \cos 30^{\circ}$ are equal and acting in opposite directions, hence cancel out each other. Then, the resultant force on the mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ at $O$,
$F=F_{O P}-\left(F_{O Q} \sin 30^{\circ}+F_{O R} \sin 30^{\circ}\right)$
$\Rightarrow F=G-\left(\dfrac{G}{2}+\dfrac{G}{2}\right) \Rightarrow F=0$ ( zero )

question:
Every planet revolves around the sun in an elliptical orbit.
A. The force acting on a planet is inversely proportional to square of distance from sun
B. Force acting on planet is inversely proportional to product of the masses of the planet and the sun.
C. The centripetal force acting on the planet is directed away from the sun and directly proportional to cube of semi - major axis of elliptical orbit.
D. The square of time period of revolution of planet is proportional to cube of semi - major axis
Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
explanation:
Force, $F=\dfrac{G M_{s} m_{e}}{r^{2}}$
So, $F \propto M_{s} m_{e}$,
$F \propto \dfrac{1}{r^{2}}$
And according to Kepler's third law or law of time period
$T^{2} \propto a^{3}$
So, correct option is (1).

Assertion: A force acts upon the earth revolving in a circular orbit about the sun and the work done on the earth due to this force is zero.

Reason: The necessary centripetal force for circular motion of earth, comes from the gravitational force between earth and sun.
explanation: It is closed path (orbit) with conservative force. The gravitational force between the Earth and the Sun provides the necessary centripetal force for the Earth's orbit around the Sun. Since the force is acting at right angles to the direction of motion, no work is done on the Earth. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

explanation: $\vec{F}_{G A}=\dfrac{G(2 m)(2 m)}{1^{2}} \hat{j}$,    https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955911/preptracker/questions/q_35551_explanation_1764955910980.png   $\begin{aligned}\vec{F}_{G B} & =\dfrac{G(m)(2 m)}{1^{2}}\left(\hat{i} \cos 30^{\circ}-\hat{j} \sin 30^{\circ}\right) \\\vec{F}_{G C} & =\dfrac{G(m)(2 m)}{1^{2}}\left(-\hat{i} \cos 30^{\circ}-\hat{j} \sin 30^{\circ}\right) \\\vec{F}_{G} & =\vec{F}_{G A}+\vec{F}_{G B}+\vec{F}_{G C} \\& =4 G m^{2} \hat{j}-2 G m^{2}\left(2 \sin 30^{\circ}\right) \hat{j}=2 G m^{2} \hat{j} .\end{aligned}$

question:
A research satellite of mass $200\,kg$ circle the Earth in an orbit of average radius $\dfrac{3 R}{2}$.
Assuming the gravitational pull of mass of $1\,kg$ on earth's surface to be $10\,N$, the pull on the satellite will be:
explanation:
$m g=10 \Rightarrow g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$
$g^{\prime}=g \dfrac{R^{2}}{r^{2}}=10 \times \dfrac{R^{2}}{(3 R / 2)^{2}}=\dfrac{40}{9}$
Pull on satellite $ = {m^\prime }{g^\prime } = 200 \times \frac{{40}}{9} = 889\,N.$

question: From a sphere of mass $M$ and radius $R$, a smaller sphere of radius $\frac{R}{2}$ is carved out such that the cavity made in the original sphere is between its centre and the periphery. (See figure). For the configuration in the figure where the distance between the centre of the original sphere and the removed sphere is 3$R$, the gravitational force between the two spheres is :-
explanation:
Let $P$ is the centre of the smaller sphere as shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951450/preptracker/questions/q_35245_explanation_1764951450370.png The field at $P$ by the big sphere having cavity is $P$. We can assume the big sphere having cavity as a combination of solid sphere and (-)$ve$ mass sphere of radius $R / 2$
$\begin{aligned}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{P} & =\vec{E}_{\text {Solid sphere }}+\vec{E}_{\text {negative mass }} \\\vec{E}_{P} & =\dfrac{-G M}{(3 R)^{2}} \hat{i}+\dfrac{G(M / 8)}{\left(\dfrac{5 R}{2}\right)^{2}} \hat{i} \\\vec{E}_{P} & =\dfrac{-G M}{9 R^{2}} \hat{i}+\dfrac{G M}{50 R^{2}} \hat{i}=\dfrac{-41 G M}{450 R^{2}} \hat{i}\end{aligned}$
So force on the smaller sphere present at $P$ is
$\vec{F}=\vec{E}_{P} \dfrac{M}{8}=\dfrac{-41 G M^{2}}{3600 R^{2}} \hat{i}$

question: Two equal masses separated by a distance $d$ attract each other with a force $(F)$. If one unit of mass is transferred from one of them to the other, the force
explanation:
Let $m$ is the mass of each body
$F=\dfrac{G m^{2}}{d^{2}}$
After transferring unit mass
$F^{\prime}=\dfrac{G(m+1)(m-1)}{d^{2}}=\dfrac{G\left(m^{2}-1\right)}{d^{2}}=\dfrac{G m^{2}}{d^{2}}-\dfrac{G}{d^{2}}$
So the force decreases by $\dfrac{G}{d^{2}}$

question: Three identical point massess, each of mass 1$kg$ lie in the $x-y$ plane at points $(0,0),(0,0.2\,m)$  and $(0.2\,m,0)$. The net gravitational force on the mass at the origin is
explanation:
Let particle $A$ lies at origin, particle $B$ and $C$ on $y$ and $x$-axis respectively https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951327/preptracker/questions/q_34801_explanation_1764951326670.png ${\overrightarrow F _{AC}} = \frac{{G{m_A}{m_C}}}{{r_{AC}^2}}\hat i$
$ = \frac{{6.67 \times {{10}^{ - 11}} \times 1 \times 1}}{{{{(0.2)}^2}}}\hat i = 1.67 \times {10^{ - 9}}N\hat i$
Similarly ${{\vec F}_{AB}} = 1.67 \times {10^{ - 9}}Nj$
$\therefore$ Net force on particle $A$
$\vec{F}=\vec{F}_{A C}+\vec{F}_{A B}=1.67 \times 10^{-9} N(\hat{i}+\hat{j})$

question: Four similar particles of mass $m$ are orbiting in a circular of radius $r$ in the same angular direction because of their mutual gravitational attractive force. The angular velocity of a particle is given by
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956910/preptracker/questions/q_34790_explanation_1764956909733.png Centripetal force $=$ net gravitational force
$\begin{gathered}\Rightarrow \dfrac{m v_{0}^{2}}{r}=2 F \cos 45^{\circ}+F_{1}=\dfrac{2 G m^{2}}{2 r^{2}} \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\dfrac{G m^{2}}{4 r^{2}} \\\dfrac{m v_{0}^{2}}{r}=\dfrac{G m^{2}}{4 r^{2}}[2 \sqrt{2}+1] \\\Rightarrow v_{0}=\left[\dfrac{G m(2 \sqrt{2}+1)}{4 r}\right]^{\dfrac{1}{2}}\end{gathered}$
The angular velocity of a particle is given by
$\omega=\dfrac{v_{0}}{r}=\left[\dfrac{G m(2 \sqrt{2}+1)}{4 r^{3}}\right]^{\dfrac{1}{2}}$

question: Two satellites $A$ and $B$ of the same mass are revolving around the earth in the concentric circular orbits such that the distance of satellite $B$ from the centre of the earth is thrice as compared to the distance of the satellite $A$ from the centre of the earth. explanation:

$r_{B}=3 r_{A}$

Now $F_{A}=\dfrac{m v_{A}^{2}}{r_{A}}=\dfrac{M}{r_{A}} \dfrac{G M}{r_{A}}=\dfrac{G M m}{r_{A}^{2}}$

question: The mass density of a spherical galaxy varies as $\dfrac{K}{r}$ over a large distance $'r'$ from its centre. In that region, a small star is in a circular orbit of radius $R$. Then the period of revolution, $T$ depends on $R$ as
explanation:
According to question, mass density of a spherical galaxy varies as $\dfrac{k}{r}$
Mass, $M=\int \rho d V$
$ \Rightarrow M = \int\limits_0^R {\frac{k}{r}4\pi {r^2}dr} $
$ \Rightarrow M = 4\pi k\int\limits_0^R {rdr} $
or, $M = 2\pi k{R^2}$
The Centripetal force is
$\begin{gathered}F_{G}=\dfrac{G M m}{R^{2}}=m \omega^{2} R \\\Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{G 2 \pi k R^{2}}{R^{2}}=\omega^{2} R \Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \pi K G}{R}} \\\therefore \quad T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=\dfrac{2 \pi \sqrt{R}}{\sqrt{2 \pi K G}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \pi R}{K G}} \Rightarrow T^{2}=\dfrac{2 \pi R}{K G}\end{gathered}$
$\because 2\pi $, $K$ and $G$ are constants
$\therefore T^{2} \propto R \text {. }$

question: The distance of the centres of moon and earth is $D$. The mass of earth is 81 times the mass of the moon. At what distance from the centre of the earth, the gravitational force on a particle will be zero
explanation:
Force will be zero at the point of zero intensity https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951245/preptracker/questions/q_34307_explanation_1764951244782.png
$\frac{{G{m_1}}}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{{G{m_2}}}{{{{\left( {d - x} \right)}^2}}}$
${\left( {d - x} \right)^2} = {x^2}\frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1}}}$
$d - x = x\frac{{\sqrt {{m_2}} }}{{\sqrt {{m_1}} }}$
$ \Rightarrow x + x\frac{{\sqrt {{m_2}} }}{{\sqrt {{m_1}} }} = d$
$x\left[ {1 + \frac{{\sqrt {{m_2}} }}{{\sqrt {{m_1}} }}} \right] = d$
$x = \frac{{\sqrt {{m_1}} }}{{\sqrt {{m_1}} + \sqrt {{m_2}} }}\,d$
$ = \frac{{\sqrt {81M} }}{{\sqrt {81M} + \sqrt M }}D = \frac{9}{{10}}D$

question: The mass of earth is 81 times the mass of moon and the distance between their centres is $R$. The distance from the centre of the earth, where gravitational force will be zero is
explanation:
Let $x$ be the distance of the point from the centre of earth, where gravitational force is zero. The gravitational field intensities due to earth and moon will have same magnitudes at that point.
Hence, $\dfrac{G \times(81 m)}{(x)^{2}}=\dfrac{G \times(m)}{(R-x)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{81}{(x)^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{(R-x)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow 81(R-x)^{2}-x^{2}=0 \Rightarrow(9 R-8 x)(9 R-10 x)=0$
$\Rightarrow x=\dfrac{9 R}{10}, \text { and } x=\dfrac{9 R}{8}$
